There are three different things going on here, which need different solutions:

* pytest `-sv` is _loud_. If you run pytest without those arguments the volume of output is less. You might also want to use `--tb=short`
* When pytest creates tests using the `yield` method being used in gabbi, it's name for the test is pretty complex (as you've seen), but that's just the way pytest works and is not something gabbi can really control (until we switch away from the yield style)
* The output of the test failure (when using `--tb=short`) is effectively the same when using pytest as it is when using unittest to run the gabbi tests. It sounds like you don't like this level of verbosity, or somehow it is not serving what you need. Can you give me an example of what you think it should look like? From there perhaps we can figure something out. At the moment the output is intentionally quite noisy (for example if the status code is not what is expected, than part of the body of the response is provided, in case that helps determining the reason for the failure).

As you probably saw, gdbgui uses `-var-update --all-values *` to update the values of all expressions every time the program pauses. gdb documents its machine interface at https://sourceware.org/gdb/onlinedocs/gdb/GDB_002fMI-Variable-Objects.html. The two relevant pieces from this documentation are below.

It looks like the right way to do this is to run `-var-set-update-range <varname> <numchildren> <new_num_children>` whenever `has_more` is true.

> ‘has_more’
> This is an integer value, which will be 1 if there are more children available outside the varobj’s update range.


> The -var-set-update-range command
> 
> Synopsis
> 
>  -var-set-update-range name from to
> Set the range of children to be returned by future invocations of -var-update.
> 
> from and to indicate the range of children to report. If from or to is less than zero, the range is reset and all children will be reported. Otherwise, children starting at from (zero-based) and up to and excluding to will be reported.
>
> Example
> 
> (gdb)
> -var-set-update-range V 1 2
> ^done
>

Second Version:
Normal text defines existing behaviour. Bold text highlights areas subject to discussion.

This text is an overview for Kernel.run() describing modes of operation.
It is expected to provide a basis for documentation as well as a general
picture for possible removal of daemon tasks.

Kernel is a pool of tasks. `run(coro=None, timeout=None)` and `spawn(coro)`
add tasks to this pool.

`coro` is the main parameter for the `run()` function. `timeout` is behaviour modifier
determining max time for polling ready tasks.

Presence or absence a `coro` leads to two major modes of operation.
Regardless of the mode, each call to `run()` processes all ready tasks until
it returns. Additionally it runs as follows:

1) `run(timeout=None) -> return None`
a) `timeout==None`: Run ready tasks and return. If there is None, wait until
the next. Process it. Return. (OneShot-Blocking)
b) `timeout==0`: Just run ready tasks if there are any. Return. (Non-blocking)
c) `timeout>0`: Like (a). But wait at most x secs. (Timeout-Blocking)

2) `run(coro, timeout=None, cancel_on_timeout=True) -> return result` / **None**
a) `timeout==None`: Run until task is done. Return result. (Hard-Blocking).
This is traditional run() **PLUS other tasks may still remain in the task pool, to be processed on next runs.**
b) `timeout==0`: **Add the task to the pool and process ready tasks. Return None.**
c) `timeout>0`: Like (a). But wait at most x secs.
**TimeoutError raised to the caller of run() if the result is not ready.** Task will be canceled **OR it will remain in the task pool depending on `cancel_on_timeout` param.**

Examples:
TODO: Provide examples for above.

Proposal: Stepped OneShot driving until all done:
```python
while kernel.tasks:
   kernel.run()
```

Are you sure that its implementation is a piece of cake?

The theoretical analysis is pretty simple: if the peer is fast enough that
every time we go to write, there is room in the send buffer, and every time
we go to read, there is data waiting in the receive buffer, then our
read/write loop will never relinquish control.
.
In this case running on my laptop, the vagaries of scheduling mean that
sometimes one of the clients will get delayed enough that it fails to keep
the server's buffers primed, which is why we do eventually see the server
switch tasks. But if anything I'm actually more worried about more
realistic situations: if the client and server are on different machines
then that potentially lets the client be scheduled *more* smoothly. Ditto
if the machines in question are more powerful with cores to spare. And if
the server is more complicated than a simple echo server, so there are
longer delays in between reads and writes while it does actually work, then
that also makes the race condition easier to hit.
.
In the HTTP/1 server case, it would look like: (1) client sends a bunch of
requests. These are short, and you can stack up quite a few of them in the
server's receive buffer. (2) server takes them out one at a time, and for
each one thinks about it a bit and then sends a longish response. (3)
client reads each response as it comes, continuously draining the server's
send buffer. So basically, as long as individual responses are small enough
to fit in the send buffer (which is not hard, Linux will auto-tune this
buffer up to multiple megabytes on fast connections), and so long as the
client can read and ack each response before the server has finished
generating the next one, then this client will starve out other clients,
potentially completely and indefinitely and deterministically.

converts the file "input.wav" to an mp3 file called "output.mp3"

```
import sox
tfm = sox.Transformer()
tfm.set_output_format(file_type='ogg')
tfm.build("input.wav", "output")
```
converts the file "input.wav" to an (extensionless) ogg file called "output"

finally,
```
import sox
tfm = sox.Transformer()
tfm.set_output_format(file_type='flac')
tfm.build("input.wav", "output.wav")
```
this works, and rather unintuitively converts "input.wav" to a flac file called "output.wav". Obviously not recommended, but possible.

    A testcase is created by subclassing unittest.TestCase. The three individual tests are
    defined with methods whose names start with the letters test. This naming convention
    informs the test runner about which methods represent tests.

[Your metaclass](https://github.com/wikimedia/pywikibot-core/blob/master/tests/aspects.py#L1442) subclasses `unittest.TestCase` (via several intermediate subclasses), which by definition makes your MetaClass a testcase according to built-in unittest.

        explicit object_detector (

        );

after that result detector can be serialized into "object_detector.dat" file and loaded/used later (even from python)

I agree that this should be implemented guizero wide, a number of widgets already support changing their color usually by the `bg` (background) property or specific `color` properties (e.g. PushButton.text_color).

Natively tkinter works with #rgb so it makes sense that guizero uses those values.

So, how to make it simpler and support the requirement...

I see 2 options:

1. create a color utility within guizero to allow you to easily create colors which guizero understands e.g.

```
from guizero import Color, App
a = App()
a.bg = Color(red, green, blue)
```

A `Color` helper could be intelligent with its input and also accept different variations:

e.g:

```
a.bg = Color("pink")
a.bg = Color("#ffffff")
# or any other variation you could think of
```  

2. The individual setters are capable of accepting other colors formats :

e.g:

```
a.bg = (red, green, blue)
a.bg = pink
a.bg = "#ffffff" 
```

Its worth noting that if you were to get a color from guizero it would still be represented as #rgb

I would favour option 2.

Looking at Waffle specifically, I think it should implement:

 - a `bg` property which will get or set the background.
 - set_pixel - could accept a color as either a single text variable or as a tuple of (red, green, blue). set_pixel would integrate the color variable at run time and if found to be a tuple convert it to a `#rgb`

This way you create a flexible interface and the user doesnt have to be explicit.

    exit()

netmiko_exceptions = (netmiko.ssh_exception.NetMikoTimeoutException,
                      netmiko.ssh_exception.NetMikoAuthenticationException)

username, password = mytools.get_credentials()

with open(sys.argv[1]) as cmd_file:
    commands = cmd_file.readlines()

with open(sys.argv[2]) as dev_file:
     devices = json.load(dev_file)

for device in devices:
    device['username'] = username
    device['password'] = password
    try:
        print('~' * 79)

        for command in commands:
            print('## Output of ' + command)
            print(connection.send_command(command))
            time.sleep(2)
            print()
        connection.disconnect()
    except netmiko_exceptions as e:
        print('Failed to ', device['ip'], e)

OK, I have a version that I consider final in master right now. It is tested working on both my Linux and Windows 7 machines. The code is here: https://github.com/mahmoud/boltons/blob/master/boltons/fileutils.py#L237

It uses ReplaceFile and adds `atomic_rename` and `replace` to the public API of `fileutils`. The semantics of `replace` are meant to be almost the same as 3.3+ `os.replace`. Except that according to our research ReplaceFile is better than MoveFileEx, so `fileutils.replace` may be superior.

Take a look and please, comment!

Almost 30%, that's great! I don't feel great about removing those value checks, however. `callable()` takes less than a tenth of a microsecond on the machines I've tested. And builtins that take keys (like `sorted()`) raise errors on passing uncallables (as well as non-iterables), so I'd definitely like to retain that behavior.

Another weakness of this approach is that the resulting dictionary must be copied in order to return a plain dictionary. For small dictionaries (like even/odd in the example provided) this is fine, but it can be a problem for larger ones. Would a normal try/except be faster for this case? Thanks again!

@sigmavirus24 yeah I could see it going either way, but I do slightly prefer the lowercase. It's meant to be a primitive, like frozenset. That said, other folks I've shopped it around to have pointed at OrderedDict and UserDict (and even boltons's own OrderedMultiDict), so I think the TitleCase may have to stay. Tell ya what though, I'd totally be down with adding a lowercased alias, what do you think of that?

@kurtbrose I think that other approaches just use method wrappers that raise errors. The compile overhead observation is a very good one, and the frozenset approach is a great idea! @induane what do you think?

I don't know how to debug this. Usually when these errors occur whatever
way the person installed the package (such as pip) installs to a different
Python distribution than whichever one they are currently running. I don't
have a Mac, but if I recall correctly it comes with a default installation.
People then install anaconda or something, and then the environments can be
messed up. So, see where pip (or whatever you used) installed filterpy, and
then look at your pythonpath for the environment you are currently running
in, and see if you are correctly pointing to the path for filterpy. For
example, I've gotten reports several times where somebody did an install,
it went to their Python 2 installation, then they ran Python 3 and filterpy
was not to be found. Running pip associated with their Python 3.x install
fixed the problem.

I think your issue is on how you reporting. By default the logging module uses a root Logger object (available at `logging.root`) that prints information to the screen. `logging.info` simply passes the message to this root object. However, by default the root object ignores all logs less important than warnings, so your info messages get dropped.

If you want to see info messages simply set the level of the root logger: `logging.root.setLevel(0)`. Or, if you are using Python3, replace `logging.info` with a simple `print` for a quick and dirty replacement.

For testing hotkeys I suggest using a simple:

```
import keyboard
keyboard.add_hotkey('shift+h', print, ['pressed'])
input()
```

Note to self. 
Linux uses IFF_BROADCAST and IFF_POINTOPOINT flags to distinguish the meaning of `ifa_broadaddr` struct:
http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getifaddrs.3.html
FreeBSD has two (less confusing) separate `ifa_broadaddr` and `ifa_dstaddr` structs:
https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=getifaddrs
OSX uses `ifa_dstaddr`:
https://developer.apple.com/library/ios/documentation/System/Conceptual/ManPages_iPhoneOS/man3/getifaddrs.3.html
Solaris uses `ifa_broadaddr` and `ifa_dstaddr` structs similarly to FreeBSD:
http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E26502_01/html/E29035/getifaddrs-3socket.html

This is mirable work, thanks a lot for doing this. As for refactoring, to me it looks like _pscygwin.py has more code in common with _pslinux.py rather than _pswindows.py.
As such, what I would recommend is to (whenever possible) keep Linux and Cygwin logic in _pslinux.py and use `if CYGWIN: ...` where necessary and when the logic differs too much just put it in _pscygwin.py. Something like this:

```
# _pscygwin.py

from . import _pslinux

# same logic
pids = _pslinux.pids

# new logic
def disk_io_counters():
      ...  
```

The trick is to be cautious about code which is executed at import time in _pslinux.py. Again, you can just use `if not CYGWIN: ...`, e.g.:

```
# _pslinux.py

if LINUX:
     PAGESIZE = os.sysconf("SC_PAGE_SIZE")
```

As for the C extension module, things may be more hairy. I took a look at your _psutil_cygwin.c and it seems it was a copy & paste of _psutil_windows.c with a lot of subsequent chances. I see a lot of differences but it looks like you moved a lot of stuff around (for no good reason?) but the main bits are pretty much the same, so I can't properly comment about that and would like to hear your input about it. 
Is it feasible to use the original _psutil_windows.c and use `#ifdef __CYGWIN__` where necessary?

1. Nice to hear that you create your own seed-calculating-function. I never expect anyone to understand that part of the docs. :smile:

2. I wouldn't have one class to cover those 3 cases. I would have 3 classes to cover those 3 cases. The latter two already exist (`telepot.exception.BotWasKickedError` and `BotWasBlockedError
`). The first one you can subclass on your own. I may consider adding it later.

3. Good suggestion to have 429 too-many-messages as a separate exception (subclass of `TelegramError`). Can you show me the description and error code of that?

4. Adding that list of errors to docs is a good idea. I will do it in the foreseeable future.

5. The way `TelegramError` and its subclasses works is this: On seeing an erroneous response, telepot matches the error *description* against all subclasses' `DESCRIPTION_PATTERNS`. If a match is found, an exception of that subclass is raised. If no match, a generic `TelegramError` is raised.

    I don't see how we can do the same thing with `BadHTTPResponse` because there is not a general way to map response to subclass. What subclasses do you want to have, by the way? `InvalidToken`? What else?

    I don't like relying on a redirect to mean an `InvalidToken` because it is simply not safe to make that assumption *on the framework-level*. It is perfectly fine on the application-level. But I don't like to put it in the framework. It is not robust.

    Finally, I don't know about your project, but it seems to me, if a user supplies a token that is so clearly wrong, it is him or her who is being unreasonable. I think we have spent enough time and energy on that.

    If you want to solve the problem cleanly, you should really ask Telegram to give a more well-defined response. It is not telepot's problem.

For anyone who follows, I have set up a **simple bot program**, monitored by **[supervisord](http://supervisord.org/)** to capture any erroneous output, hopefully to get some clues on this. The setup is as follows:

Machine: **Raspberry Pi 2 Model B**
Raspbian: **Wheezy**
Python: **2.7**
telepot: **6.5**
Program: an **echo bot**, which only echoes the text message I have sent

```python
import sys
import time
import telepot

def handle(msg):
    msg_type, chat_type, chat_id = telepot.glance(msg)

    if msg_type != 'text':
        return

    text = msg['text']

    if chat_id != USER_ID:
        return

    bot.sendMessage(chat_id, text)

TOKEN = sys.argv[1]
USER_ID = long(sys.argv[2])

bot = telepot.Bot(TOKEN)
bot.notifyOnMessage(handle)
print 'Listening ...'

while 1:
    time.sleep(1)
```

In supervisord's program section, I have set `autorestart` to `false`, so the program would *not* get re-started after crashing.

As of now, it has been up for **48 hours** and is still receiving and echoing messages as expected. Except for the occasional `ConnectionError` encountered by `getUpdate` requests (which is normal), no other problems is apparent.

Stay tune for updates.

My understanding from what you are saying is the model learns from corpus with proper text which rarely has ) without a space after it. We should do preprocessing on the input, right?

If we can do that, we might want to add some preprocessing on a few other things like the following cases, maybe? Not sure we want to support that or it's a responsibility of the app to do basic cleaning. If we don't want to waste time do cleaning in the tokenization, maybe we can offer a separate function to help app developers to do preprocessing? Maybe I'm too picky :)

At least I found these:
1) LF or CR. The model knows to break other symbols like -, /, ๆ etc. but not LF/CR. E.g.  
กินเยอะๆ
หายไวๆ
Got it as ['กิน', 'เยอะๆ\n', 'หาย', 'ไว', 'ๆ']

2) mixture of Thai and English without spaces. E.g. 
คนนั่งโต๊ะโน้นหน้าเหมือนjohnny deppเลยหวะ
['คน', 'นั่ง', 'โต๊ะ', 'โน้น', 'หน้า', 'เหมือ', 'น', 'johnny', ' ', 'depp', 'เลย', 'หวะ']

Well, we could also have 'jonny depp' together as a named entity but I guess we don't need to concern much about segmentation of English words as it is out of scope. At least preprocessing to add spaces between Thai and English boundaries can help the Thai tokenization to come out as 'เหมือน' instead of 'เหมือ', 'น'.

Adding a timeout for MTU after adding the veth interface seems  to work around the issue.

```python
#!/usr/bin/python3

# Imports.
import pyroute2
import time

# Constants.
RANGE = 256

# Functions.
def remove(name):
    if name in ipdb.by_name.keys():
        print("removing %s" % name)
        ipdb.interfaces[name].remove().commit()

def cleanup():
    for i in range(RANGE):
        testbr_name = "testbr%i" % i
        testgre_name = "testgre%i" % i
        testvp0_name = "testv%ip0" % i

        remove(testbr_name)
        remove(testgre_name)
        remove(testvp0_name)

# Setup.
# Only happens if created in rootns.
ipdb = pyroute2.IPDB()

cleanup()

# Add interfaces.
for i in range(RANGE):
    testbr_name = "testbr%i" % i
    testgre_name = "testgre%i" % i
    testvp0_name = "testv%ip0" % i
    testvp1_name = "testv%ip1" % i

    print("creating %s" % testgre_name)
    testgre = ipdb.create(
        ifname=testgre_name,
        kind="gretap",
        gre_local="192.0.2.1",
        gre_remote="192.0.2.2",
        gre_ttl=16,
        gre_ikey=i,
        gre_okey=i,
        gre_iflags=32,
        gre_oflags=32,
    )
    ipdb.commit()

    print("creating %s" % testvp0_name)
    testvp0 = ipdb.create(ifname=testvp0_name, kind="veth", peer=testvp1_name)
    ipdb.commit()

    print("creating %s" % testbr_name)
    testbr = ipdb.create(ifname=testbr_name, kind="bridge")
    ipdb.commit()

    print("adding port for %s to %s" % (testgre_name, testbr_name))
    testbr.add_port(testgre)
    # testbr.add_port(testgre.name)
    # testbr.add_port(testgre.index)
    ipdb.commit()

    print("adding port for %s to %s" % (testvp0_name, testbr_name))
    testbr.add_port(testvp0)
    # testbr.add_port(testvp0.name)
    # testbr.add_port(testvp0.index)
    if testbr["mtu"] == 1500:
        print("waiting for mtu")
        waited = 0.0
        while testbr["mtu"] != 1458 and waited < 1.0:
            waited += 0.01
            time.sleep(0.01)
    ipdb.commit()

    print("bringing up %s" % testgre_name)
    testgre.up()
    ipdb.commit()

    print("bringing up %s" % testvp0_name)
    testvp0.up()
    ipdb.commit()

    print("bringing up %s" % testgre_name)
    testbr.up()
    ipdb.commit()

# Remove interfaces.
cleanup()

# Teardown.
ipdb.release()
```

I think we've figured this out.  Not sure if you'd consider it a bug or not?  We were performing long running "batch" operations inside `on_partitions_assigned` of our `ConsumerRebalanceListener` subclass.  The first call to `on_partitions_assigned` happens on the first poll and apparently before the `HeartbeatTask` is `scheduled` to run; I don't believe the heartbeat task gets scheduled until after `on_partitions_assigned` returns .  Thus... as we poll no heartbeats are ever send.

Our solution is to remove any long running "batch" operations from the `ConsumerRebalanceListener` listeners and run them from within the main poll loop.  The `ConsumerRebalanceListener` listeners now set a flag that the main poll loop inspects to signal a "batch" operation.  This allows any internal "housekeeping" that is associated with a rebalance to occur before attempting to run the "batch" job.  To keep the heartbeat active during a long running batch we 1) pause all partitions and then 2) regularly call `poll` with a `timeout_ms` of 100 (a `timeout_ms` of 0 or a value that is too small doesn't result a heartbeat request ???).  We can also abort batch jobs so if a rebalance happens during a batch it's immediately aborted and restarted.  Generally seems to be working.

How kafka-python decides to send a heartbeat is a mystery and very complicated.  There should be a predictable and transparent way to get the heartbeat request to be transmitted.  The heartbeat is so integral to using automatic partition assignment you need to understand how to make it happen predictably.

```python
from whitenoise.middleware import WhiteNoiseMiddleware


class CustomWhiteNoise(WhiteNoiseMiddleware):
    """Adds support for serving index pages for directory paths."""

    INDEX_NAME = 'index.html'

    def update_files_dictionary(self, *args):
        super(CustomWhiteNoise, self).update_files_dictionary(*args)
        index_page_suffix = '/' + self.INDEX_NAME
        index_name_length = len(self.INDEX_NAME)
        for url, static_file in self.files.items():
            if url.endswith(index_page_suffix):
                # For each index file found, add a corresponding URL->content mapping
                # for the file's parent directory, so that the index page is served for
                # the bare directory URL ending in '/'.
                parent_directory_url = url[:-index_name_length]
                self.files[parent_directory_url] = static_file

    def find_file(self, url):
        # In debug mode, find_file() is used to serve files directly from the filesystem
        # instead of using the list in `self.files`, so we append the index filename so
        # that will be served if present.
        if url.endswith('/'):
            url += self.INDEX_NAME
        return super(CustomWhiteNoise, self).find_file(url)
```

Your example doesn't check the hash code though, which is half the point. The YOLO switch is meant to reduce the number of comparisons required by offloading half the comparisons to the hash function and the other half to `__eq__` like a bloom filter. I wrote a blog post about this actually [here](http://blog.kevmo314.com/python-dictionary-speed-hacks.html). The reason there's a switch to enable this is because when YOLO is enabled, it violates python's definition of `__eq__`, which is not desirable. It conveniently exploits some duplicate execution behavior in python to improve performance.

I did notice that `self._wrap_text` was missing from both `__eq__` and `__hash__` so that's actually a bug, but the general principle of only checking half the properties is intentional by design.

The WAI spec http://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria/roles#row states that if you apply role="row" to a tr, then it must contain columnheader, gridcell, and/or rowheader elements (the "required owned elements"). Columnheader is for th within thead, gridcell is for td within tbody, and rowheader is for th within tbody. I believe that once you apply role="grid" to a table you are required to apply the proper roles to all table elements within it.

However - and here is where my understanding gets a bit fuzzy - I also think that if you do not intend for the cells themselves to be keyboard-navigable, as with a spreadsheet, for example, you do not necessarily need to apply role="grid" to your table. It almost seems like default tablesorter behavior could be to leave out the grid roles altogether, with an add-on widget to provide that sort of behavior if desired. There are many references to role="grid" applying to "interactive" tables, and tablesorter certainly produces interactive tables, but in its most basic function of re-sorting the table rows, it may not be a complex enough interaction to require a grid. But.. I'm really not sure about that.

I can say that to meet the requirements of the people doing accessibility testing for our government customers, the tabindex and nicely-updating aria-label that you've added to header elements are good enough for simple tables; we got dinged (on a previous tablesorter version) for not having accessible sorting controls, so I was very thrilled to get them on our next tablesorter update! I'm not even sure that they would find this problem with grid roles; to my knowledge they are using the Web Accessibility Toolbar for IE http://www.paciellogroup.com/resources/wat/ , which takes a lot more human interaction than the nice, concise report from the axe library. You can use the WAT to show elements that have ARIA roles, but I haven't yet found a tool in there that makes judgement calls on whether or not you're doing it right (though it may be there and I just haven't found it yet).

It is possible that the experience of a sortable table for an actual screen reader user is better with full grid markup, but I couldn't say for sure.

I'm not sure if that helps or not, and now I need to go to a meeting. :-) The one clear thing, though, is that once you start down the path of role="grid", all table markup needs an appropriate grid-related role.

Good point, I'm thinking it should only be at the top level rather than in each source/target collection like I showed. So all cases within the named group use the same configuration environment and the name of the group gives some indication to that configuration. 

So within en.yml or en/something.yml we would have appropriately-named test groups each with the ability to set configuration options for all test cases in the group. Anchors make this quite readable and I am leaning toward en/something.yml structure rather than a single file per language to keep the data grouped similarly to the current test classes.

Here is an example of a few test cases for weekdays and the `DOWParseStyle` flag:
```yaml
constants:
    - &tuesday 2016-01-19 00:00:00
    - &wednesday 2016-01-20 00:00:00
    - &thursday 2016-01-21 00:00:00
    - &next_wednesday 2016-01-27 00:00:00
    - &wednesday_phrases
        - "Wednesday"
        - "wed"
        - "Wed."

weekdays:
    cases:
        - source_time: *tuesday
          phrases: *wednesday_phrases
          target: *wednesday
        # Since the Wednesday of this week has passed we should get the
        # Wednesday of the following week
        - source_time: *thursday
          phrases: *wednesday_phrases
          target: *next_wednesday

weekdays_dow_parse_style_0:
    options: 
        dow_parse_style: 0
    cases:
        - source_time: *tuesday
          phrases: *wednesday_phrases
          target: *wednesday
        # The Wednesday of this week has passed but the parse style should
        # give us that day anyway
        - source_time: *thursday
          phrases: *wednesday_phrases
          target: *wednesday
```

The named anchors make the test cases read nicely without getting bogged down in the specific dates. This parses to:

Just to clarify that I do not misunderstand - - you are proposing that ``term.bold_blue('blessings 2.0')`` returns Sequence instance instead of unicode/str, and the convenience methods would be used naturally from there? Such as ``term.bold_blue('blessings 2.0').center()``

I think I probably experiment with this initially, and immediately hit a wall with pickling -- which made me take note of how "heavy" i just made a unicode string: bringing all of the ``term`` instance along with a unicode string currently returned. I was more comfortable with Sequence used only internally by the convenience methods of Terminal, and not encouraging developers to have to know about it. Especially, as you say, what happens with ``__add__``, which is very commonly done. For pickling, it would have to reduce to a plain unicode string by implementing ``__deepcopy__``, dropping its internal reference to a Terminal instance. I think it could go surprisingly bad in edge cases. It still won't build but hopefully no one else on earth is using this version anymore.

After thinking about this a bit more and studying how DBI uses the ODBC API, I believe the behaviour you're seeing is [because you haven't consumed all the resultsets the stored procedure generated](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/issues/262). The reason why it seems to work without the locks is because there are fewer resultsets generated than will fill the write buffer, and why it works with the locks in SSMS and DBI is because they automatically read all the generated resultsets. To test this theory, you can (recommended) add [`SET NOCOUNT ON`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/t-sql/statements/set-nocount-transact-sql) inside your SP at the beginning --- before all the other statements, and/or add this after the execute to read all the pending resultsets (you will have to use cursor.execute() to get an explicit cursor handle):

```
while cursor.nextset():
    pass
```

When autocommit is False, which is the default and what is usually recommended if you are making updates, the database begins a transaction when the first statement is executed.  You can look up ODBC's autocommit handling to for details - pyodbc does not do anything special.  That is, it does not call begin transaction for you - the DB/driver handles it.

I did some additional tests, and found some additional issues:

## 3.4.0

    mlaunch --replicaset --sharded 2 --auth

Cannot shutdown (`mlaunch stop`) due to auth user not added on shards. User only added on the config servers.

    mlaunch --sharded 3 --single

`mlaunch` doesn't exit due to csrs not initiated. Manually calling `rs.initiate()` on the csrs allows `mlaunch` to continue.

## 3.2.11

    mlaunch --replicaset --sharded 2 --auth

Cannot shutdown (`mlaunch stop`) due to auth user not added on shards. User only added on the config servers.

    mlaunch --sharded 2 --replicaset --csrs --storageEngine mmapv1

Crashed trying to initiate csrs due to config servers not using WiredTiger.

    mlaunch --sharded 2 --replicaset --config 1 --csrs

The --csrs setting is ignored, resulting cluster is sccc.

## 3.0.14

    mlaunch --replicaset --sharded 2 --auth

Cannot shutdown (`mlaunch stop`) due to auth user not added on shards. User only added on the config servers.

The internals changed a while back to extract a new `SlidePart` class from the `Slide` class. The `.rels` attribute moved over with the `SlidePart` object. The `SlidePart` object for a slide is accessed using the `.part` property on the `Slide` object.

So where you previously would have `slide.rels` you would now need `slide.part.rels`. I believe in the code above the change would be `source.rels` -> `source.part.rels` and `dest.rels` -> `dest.part.rels`.

* it might be nice to have more than one for some modules eg sysdata and also have the config used

The scaling is relative to the size of the figure and the frequency of the words. The frequencies are normalized against the max frequency, so the absolute values are irrelevant. If you add more text to the large one with values higher than 5%, the 5% can become arbitrary small because of the space fitting algorithm. While the 5% in the subfigure will always be max_font_size big because it's the word with the largest frequency.

If you want corresponding font-sizes, you can get the font-size of the 5% from the bigger figure (it's stored in the wordcloud) and use it as max_font for the smaller figure.

It works, but it requires setting a parameter for every object. I often use IPy for quick scripts, especially to do block aggregation, so this turns a simple [s.add(IP(foo)) for foo in f.readlines()] into an ugly block.

I also think it breaks the contract that 'wantprefixlen' sets. Since the default is to display the prefix length if it's not a host, there's no difference between 'None' and '1'. IMO consistent output is desirable when the output format is specifically requested to include the prefix, which means always outputting the full prefix if that's what I ask for.

If you think it makes more sense to pass NoPrefixForSingleIP in the constructor of IP, that could be another option that works for me, but in my opinion there's no need to maintain this as a separate instance variable, it can just be a different behaviour for wantprefixlen.

For some context, I am often using IPy to generate prefix lists or routes to be imported into routers/routing software. Many platforms will assume an unqualified IP is a /32 (/128), but some require you to be explicit.

You can write the config file once to a known location on your (non-container) computer:
``` shell
telegram-send --configure --config ~/telegram-send.conf
```
Then copy that file to a known location in the container, I recommend `/etc/telegram-send.conf`. Then within the container you use `telegram-send --config /etc/telegram-send.conf` and whatever options/messages you'd like to send. I recommend this particular config location because you can then use `telegram-send -g` within the container, because the `-g` options defaults the config location to `/etc/telegram-send.conf`.

I hacked up a patch that doesn't break Python 2. Python 2 never had an issue with invalid characters for the current encoding, as `str` isn't decoded in Python 2.

This is pretty ugly stuff. Can probably see why I want to avoid it. See the earlier discussion for why `encoding="utf8"` turns ugly for Python 2, even if `io.open()` is used.

```diff
diff --git a/kconfiglib.py b/kconfiglib.py
index 99bdf80..8fb8b7a 100644
--- a/kconfiglib.py
+++ b/kconfiglib.py
@@ -877,7 +877,7 @@ class Kconfig(object):
           would usually want it enclosed in '/* */' to make it a C comment,
           and include a final terminating newline.
         """
-        with open(filename, "w") as f:
+        with _open_utf8(filename, "w") as f:
             f.write(header)

I swear this is like the third time I've run into this, and I always forget what the actual issue is, but when rediscover it, I remember that I ran into this before.

Python3 does this:

```py
>>> sre_parse.parse_template(r'\u005cg', pattern)
([], ['\\u005cg'])
```

While Python2 does this:

```py
>>> sre_parse.parse_template(ur'\u005cg', pattern)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/sre_parse.py", line 738, in parse_template
    raise error, "missing group name"
sre_constants.error: missing group name
```

My first revision failed because when I refactored the the test case, I change the value to `r'\u005cg'` specifically to ensure that we didn't translate strings to `\g` and then run it through `sre.parse_template`.  While Python 3 does not convert the Unicode symbol to an actual character, Python 2 does.  Now I remember why I prefer Python 3.  Had we done the same refactor, but kept some other Unicode value, it would have passed.  This also reminds me why I convert characters to regex octal format when casing them and they fall below 0xFF.  Re octal notation side steps Python2 handling of character representation in raw strings.  I really should put a comment in the code to remind me next time I want to test this case.  I should probably just put a PY2 switch around the test with a comment.

Much like last time I ran into this, I have no interest in attempting to address workarounds for Python2's Unicode handling.  Just don't specify a Unicode character that doubles as a regex syntax symbol in your replace template if you are on Python 2.

At least I understand why it went to crap.  Can't wait to drop Python 2 in the future.

I finally figure out that it was some issue releated to Dropzone.js. When you enable parallel uploads, the upload input name will become something like `param[index_number]`. For example, I upload three file, here is the `request.files` we get eventually:
```python
ImmutableMultiDict([('param[2]', <FileStorage: u'4b83c89cf5daecd84ba4df44117a253a.jpg' ('image/jpeg')>), ('param[1]', <FileStorage: u'3fd8589b278c544dc10caaedfef218ac.jpg' ('image/jpeg')>), ('param[0]', <FileStorage: u'3b082acf752f82710b3800ec261898d4.jpg' ('image/jpeg')>)])
```
These three file's key was `param[2]`, `param[1]` and `param[0]`, that's why you can't get a list of file with the key `param` (e.g. `request.files.getlist('param')`). 

However, you can do something like this to get a list of file:
```python    
    if request.method == 'POST':
        file_list = []
        for i in range(len(request.files)):
            file_list.append(request.files.get('param[%d]' % i))
        # the file_list here is what you want
```
Besides, The configuration options should be like this:
```
...
DROPZONE_UPLOAD_MULTIPLE = True
DROPZONE_PARALLEL_UPLOADS = 3
```

After a lot of fiddling, I think I was able to reproduce the problem. Could you confirm for me: I believe that your system has the `libsodium13` package installed, but not `libsodium-dev`. Is that right?

It looks like pynacl's `setup.py` uses a cffi test to see if it can import symbols from the `libsodium.so` file, and if that works, it assumes that the header files are available too. That assumption breaks if a system has the runtime package installed but not the development package.

So it sounds like upstream pynacl's detection code needs some work (either it must search for a header file, not just the .so, or it should do a trial compilation and fall back to the bundled copy of libsodium if that fails). I'll file a bug.

And in the meanwhile, I'll add the README line, along with an explanation of the root cause.

Also, I don't think this change is correct / necessary. The `_build` function accumulates a list of things which the `_finish` function will eventually `', '.join`. Before your change:

```
>>> s.set_active_channels([0,1,2,3], [0])
DEBUG:saleae.saleae:Send >SET_ACTIVE_CHANNELS, digital_channels, 0, 1, 2, 3, analog_channels, 0<

>  but what is the difference in result between:
>
> - single ffmpeg command two pass linear EBU,
> - ffmpeg-normalize actual two pass linear EBU?

You cannot do a “single ffmpeg command two pass linear EBU” normalization. FFmpeg always performs one pass only. My tool just helps in calling ffmpeg twice, and supplying the statistics of the first run as command line arguments to the second run.

For more info, please read [this blog article](http://k.ylo.ph/2016/04/04/loudnorm.html) from the person who wrote the `loudnorm` filter, especially:

> Supplying the filter with [the first pass] stats allow it to make more intelligent normalization decisions, as well as normalize linearly if possible. […] For precise integrated loudness, always use dual-pass mode and supply the offset parameter.

I'm glad it worked well for you – happy to see that people find the tool useful!

@babbageclunk

The query `SELECT * FROM test WHERE ((/*QueryRewrite':SQL_CACHE*/1) AND id=1 )` should be rewritten. Is `django_mysql` in your `INSTALLED_APPS` as per [the installation guide](https://django-mysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html) and did you set `DJANGO_MYSQL_REWRITE_QUERIES = True` in your settings as per [query hints](https://django-mysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/queryset_extensions.html#query-hints) ?

Also the query cache is removed in MySQL 8 and is disabled by default in MariaDB ([docs](https://mariadb.com/kb/en/library/query-cache/)), so I'd recommend against building a new app that depends on it at this time - instead maybe try Django's cache framework. You can avoid running memcached by using Django-MySQL's [cache backend](https://django-mysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cache.html).

Thanks for submitting an issue. This currently works as it is by design, basing on the REST best practices. Although sending body in DELETE is not explicitly forbidden, it is not recommended.

According to [RFC 7231, "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP/1.1): Semantics and Content"](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-4.3.5):
>    A payload within a DELETE request message has no defined semantics;
>    sending a payload body on a DELETE request might cause some existing
>    implementations to reject the request.

Please create a new issue describing the real-world use-case, that possibly needs this to be compromised. For discussion on the topic, see e.g. Stack Overflow discussion on [Is an entity body allowed for an HTTP DELETE request?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/299628/is-an-entity-body-allowed-for-an-http-delete-request).

Yes the watchers trigger if either the reference to the data source changes, or its size. We don't watch for changes within item for performance reason. You can be more strict in your watcher by changing the line [here](https://github.com/lorenzofox3/Smart-Table/blob/master/src/stTable.js#L44) and use a collection watcher instead. 

Or as @yashikagarg13  suggested, overwrite the properties of a row while not changing the reference to it.

Lastly you can splice the source collection but this will likely trigger the watchers twice (as the size of the collection changes twice)

```Javascript
var temp = $scope.rowCollection.splice(index, 1); //change in size
$scope.rowCollection.splice(newIndex,0,temp); // another change in size
```

Since you're fetching attachments, and some of them may be large, I would suggest lowering the paging size. You can do that with the following:

```python
all_emails = mailbox.all()  # This is a queryset, nothing is fetched yet
all_emails.page_size = 10
for item in all_emails.iterator():
    # Do something
```

You also have the option of handling messages with attachments separately by filtering on `has_attachments=True|False`, or you can handle very large messages separately by filtering on the `size` field, which is the total item size in bytes.

You could also optimize your script a bit by only fetching the fields you actually need. By default we get everything under the sun. Since you want attachments and the body, which usually contain the most data, there's not a *lot* to gain, but you can at least avoid `mime_content`. Something like `all_emails = mailbox.all().only('subject', 'body', 'attachments')`

Another possibility is to create an `EffectiveRights` object that subclasses `EWSElement` and defines the 7 fields in `FIELDS`, and a then create a custom field that subclasses `EWSElementField` and in the `clean()` method lets the user either set an `EffectiveRights` instance or a list of simple strings.

`add` is defined in `changeform_view` [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/admin/options.py#L1421) and the distinguishers (for *state*, rather than *view*, but principally accomplishes the same) are added to the context in `render_change_form` [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/admin/options.py#L1040-L1041).

Within the revision/revert views, these are always `add=False, change=True` as far as I can see. While conceptually for revert it might actually make more sense for both `add/change` to be `False`, there is probably already style/template changes in the wild which work based on the value of those being what they are (because after all the reversion views re-use the extant changeform view) so by adding `revert/revision=True` to the context available, you're enabling further refinement both at the template level, and at the subclass and middleware levels by doing different things based on what's in the `TemplateResponse` context data, potentially before it is rendered. I can't say whether anyone would actually want such a thing (other than me) though.

If you can't upgrade to the latest reversion, then just import Revision and
Version from reversion.models, and use the standard Django ORM to filter
and delete old revisions.

It's clear now the rationale of the decision and it makes sense. Technology moves so fast that decisions that once were natural suddenly becomes not obvious anymore...

The system I'm deploying to is a fairly new machine running CentOS 7 (Linux Kernel 3.10). As I understood from what I've read [here](http://www.2uo.de/myths-about-urandom/) and in the article mentioned before, in this kind of environment, it would be potentially unsafe to use `/dev/urandom` in two cases: 

1. During the first boot of the machine with freshly installed OS;

2. If it's a VM that was cloned or rewinded to a previous state.

None of those is my case so I have no reason to think `/dev/urandom` is deterministic on that server.

Back to Knox design, introducing a setting seems like a good idea but it may not be necessary. I did some research about `CryptGenRandom` and it seems to be the prefered way of getting cryptographically safe random bytes in Windows (as `/dev/urandom` is in *nix), as It's said [here](https://msdn.microsoft.com/pt-br/library/windows/desktop/aa379942(v=vs.85).aspx).

I'm by no means an expert neither did an extensive and carefull research on this issue but I'm pretty convinced that just using `os.random` instead of `OpenSSL.rand.bytes` would be safer and eliminate a dependency on the project.

If you find that makes sense I can submit a pull request to do that (or at least the setting thing). That'd be usefull to me as I'd be able to get my project back using the oficial version of Knox.

Ran into the same problem with this code:

```python
@sockets.route('/status.sock')
def status_sock(socket):
    app.logger.info('Websocket opened')
    with open(log_file, encoding='utf-8') as run_file:
        while not socket.closed:
            app.logger.info('Check for new data')
            new_data = run_file.read()
            if new_data:
                socket.send(new_data)
            gevent.sleep(1)
    app.logger.info('Websocket closed')  # never called
```

Everything worked fine, execpt a *slight* problem: No sockets were ever closed!

This is really an issue with the underlying gevent-websocket library. It seems like calling `socket.receive()` is *necessary* to detect if the socket is closed, but the same method is blocking..  See https://gitlab.com/noppo/gevent-websocket/issues/1

Here's my current ugly approach:

```python
@sockets.route('/status.sock')
def status_sock(socket):
    app.logger.info('Websocket opened')
    with open(log_file, encoding='utf-8') as run_file:
        while not socket.closed:
            app.logger.info('Check for new data')
            new_data = run_file.read()
            if new_data:
                socket.send(new_data)
            with gevent.Timeout(1.0, False):
                message = socket.receive()
    app.logger.info('Websocket closed')
```

@Alex891 I was able to run the job successfully ONLY after increasing the [streaming interval](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/1c38778570dd6103142af6e46d051049d2c5fc79/examples/mnist/streaming/mnist_spark.py#L26) to 60 seconds.  Apparently, in the past, it was detecting any new files in the directory, but now it seems to only detect files with timestamps in the current time window.  And, since we need to atomically `hadoop fs -mv` a file into the `stream_data` dir, this actually retains the file's original creation time... so you actually have to `hadoop fs -cp` an input file to create a new file with a recent timestamp, and then `hadoop fs -mv` that file all within the current interval period.  Obviously, this should be less of an issue once your data pipelines are all connected, but it's a bit of a pain during testing/debugging...

FWIW, you should be able to test on a single-machine Spark Standalone cluster with less hassle, as follows:
```
export TFoS_HOME=<path_to_TensorFlowOnSpark>
export MASTER=spark://$(hostname):7077
export SPARK_WORKER_INSTANCES=2
export CORES_PER_WORKER=1
export TOTAL_CORES=$((${CORES_PER_WORKER}*${SPARK_WORKER_INSTANCES}))

# start spark standalone cluster
${SPARK_HOME}/sbin/start-master.sh; ${SPARK_HOME}/sbin/start-slave.sh -c $CORES_PER_WORKER -m 3G ${MASTER}

# setup mnist data in "conjoined" CSV format (label|image)
rm -r mnist/csv2
${SPARK_HOME}/bin/spark-submit \
--master ${MASTER} \
--conf spark.cores.max=${TOTAL_CORES} \
--conf spark.task.cpus=${CORES_PER_WORKER} \
--conf spark.executorEnv.JAVA_HOME="$JAVA_HOME" \
--archives mnist/mnist.zip#mnist \
${TFoS_HOME}/examples/mnist/mnist_data_setup.py \
--output mnist/csv2 \
--format csv2

rm -rf mnist_model stream_data; mkdir stream_data
${SPARK_HOME}/bin/spark-submit \
--master ${MASTER} \
--py-files ${TFoS_HOME}/tfspark.zip,${TFoS_HOME}/examples/mnist/streaming/mnist_dist.py \
--conf spark.cores.max=${TOTAL_CORES} \
--conf spark.task.cpus=${CORES_PER_WORKER} \
--conf spark.executorEnv.JAVA_HOME="$JAVA_HOME" \
${TFoS_HOME}/examples/mnist/streaming/mnist_spark.py \
--cluster_size ${SPARK_WORKER_INSTANCES} \
--images stream_data \
--format csv2 \
--mode train \
--model mnist_model

# in another shell, copy data into the stream
export TFoS_HOME=<path_to_TensorFlowOnSpark>
cd ${TFoS_HOME}
cp mnist/csv2/train/part-00000 stream_data
cp mnist/csv2/train/part-00001 stream_data
cp mnist/csv2/train/part-0000[2-9] stream_data

# ctrl-c to stop streaming, or
# shut down streaming gracefully via:
python TensorFlowOnSpark/com/yahoo/ml/tf/reservation_client.py <host> <port>
```

Regarding other approaches, something I looked at was the following:

```python
class MacroMetric(EvalMetric):

    def __init__(self, base_metric):
        super(MacroMetric, self).__init__("macro_" + base_metric.name, output_names=base_metric.output_names,
                                          label_names=base_metric.label_names)
        self.base_metric = base_metric

    def update(self, labels, preds):
        self.base_metric.update(labels, preds)
        self.sum_metric += self.base_metric.get()[1]
        self.num_inst += 1
        self.base_metric.reset()
```

Any metric that has defined the "micro" behavior can then be used as "macro" just by calling `metric = mx.metric.MacroMetric(mx.metric.F1())`, but that seems pretty awkward for the users, and also changes the default behavior to micro, which doesn't work for backwards compatibility. The current solution works well enough, and we could probably revisit in a later PR if needed.

> In fact, zmq.POLLXXX constants can be used with loop.add_handler, because it just an INT value passed to function add_hander.

It may be a *coincidence* that these opaque constants have the same value (it's only true for POLLIN and READ, not POLLOUT and WRITE), but `add_handler(IOLoop.READ)` and `zmq_poll(zmq.POLLIN)` do not mean the same thing on a zmq socket.

> according to Tornado document: tornado.ioloop.IOLoop.add_handler, it could be used with zmq.asyncio Sockets.

It can be used, but you must be very very careful. External eventloops cannot poll zmq sockets natively, they can only poll the zmq.FD socket attribute, and zmq.POLLIN and IOLoop.READ do not mean the same thing when applied to zmq sockets. zmq `poll(s, POLLIN)` means that there are messages to be read on the socket. `add_handler(s, IOLoop.READ)` on a zmq socket only means that there *may* be an event on the socket, and `socket.EVENTS` must be checked to see whether they are POLLIN or POLLOUT events. Additionally, all events must be consumed prior to the next poll because the zmq.FD is edge-triggered, not level-triggered. Failure to do so will result in a hang, as the poller will not wake until all events have been consumed.

I don't think the annotations PEP is applicable because traits are not the types for the actual values, they instantiate the *validators* of the values. TraitTypes are not useful in themselves, except for instantiating traits, which can then be used for validating actual values. So passing `Unicode` is implicitly asking traitlets to instantiate `Unicode()` for you, rather than instantiating it explicitly yourself. The PEP describes *actual* types, for which you can use `isinstance`. This is not the case for TraitTypes.

To draw a comparison: `str` is the right annotation because it provides something useful for validating (`isinstance(name, str)`). `str()` is not, because it does not provide something useful for validating (e.g. `isinstance(name, str())` doesn't work). The same goes for TraitType, but in reverse: `Unicode` does *not* provide something useful for validating (A TraitType is useless until instantiated), while `Unicode()` does (`Unicode().validate(obj, name)`).

So while I agree that `List(str)` would be preferable to `List(str())` (if that worked here), the same logic draws the reverse conclusion about whether to instantiate TraitTypes, since TraitTypes are not the types of the values, but only the constructors for the validators.

I agree, it is probably impossible to enforce order agnosticism in `allows`. But by ignoring the result value of the validators and sending each validator the same object at least, you would at least not encourage people to rely on such behaviour. Precisely because chained coercion problems are hard to resolve, leaving that option out would make users stop first to think about what they want. If someone really needs chained coercion, then he'd probably want a well designed API for that purpose, and he'd also give a usage example to base the API design choices on. However, by offering a coercion API here, it will be used as such, and eventually an issue of coercion composition (or even composition between coercion and validation) will show up. Trying to clean-up the API at that point is nearly impossible without breaking backward compatibility. 

I'd say that most of the uses of `.allows` would remain validation. If there is indeed a need for coercion too, why not add a separate `.coerce` API for these cases? @SylcainCorlay once mentioned in a similar discussion over at traittypes that traitlets probably would have separated coercion and validation if it started all over again, but is bound by backwards compatibility now. This is a new API, so now would be the time to make such design decisions!

It seems like you already know how to discover a commit and access a tree in that commit. In order to loop through all the differences and get the files changed, here's what you can do:

```python
repo = Repository(discover_repository("/path/to/repo"))
commit = repo.revparse_single("HEAD")
diff = commit.tree.diff_to_tree()
fileschanged = []
for patch in diff:
    fileschanged.append(repo[patch.delta.new_file.oid].name)
fileschanged = set(fileschanged)
```

The search_spec.details needs to be vim.host.DatastoreBrowser.FileInfo.Details. You could have it the same as the working example.
Under the search_spec.query[0] # which is a VmDiskQuery
you can set the details there to include capacityKb/diskType/hardwareVersion/thin.

E.g.
```
search_spec = vim.host.DatastoreBrowser.SearchSpec()
queryDetails = vim.host.DatastoreBrowser.FileInfo.Details()
queryDetails.fileSize = True
queryDetails.modification = True
queryDetails.fileType = True
queryDetails.fileOwner = True
search_spec.details = queryDetails

queryDetailsDisk = vim.host.DatastoreBrowser.VmDiskQuery.Details()
queryDetailsDisk.capacityKb = True
queryDetailsDisk.diskType = True
queryDetailsDisk.hardwareVersion = True
queryDetailsDisk.thin = True
search_spec.query = [queryDetailsDisk]
```

Without seeing the website, it's not possible to say for sure, but generally speaking your problem looks quite typical. With modern websites, it's normal that elements are load dynamically with JavaScript. Because of dynamic loading, browser and therefore Selenium doesn't know when page is loaded fully. This causes that test must wait page load, in simple form, test could wait that the element is present in the page, before doing the interaction with the element (please see the different Wait... keywords in the keyword documentation for more details). There are other possibilities, like waiting that JavaScript is not running or checking internal state of the webpage. But I would start with Wait... keywords.

It is a good idea to run unit tests as an external process and not by directly calling `run_unit_tests` method. In the current approach unit tests are always executed by using the Python interpreter that runs `run_tests.py` script.

The implementation to run tests using `sys.executable` doesn't make much sense, though, because it ends up using the exact same interpreter that is used for running `run_tests.py`. There's also a problem that on Jython `sys.executable` may not always be set correctly.

A good way to accomplish the original goal would be using the given `interpreter` to run the unit tests. This should be done after the larger acceptance test cleanup done in PR #710, though. Or perhaps I could add this enhancement into that PR as well.

As @gsemet notes, the key problem for tools like `pip`/`pipenv`/`setuptools`/etc is that they're *part of bootstrapping the dependency management system*, and we can't necessarily assume that that is already working in an end users' environment, just because they have a Python install.

Debian & Ubuntu, for example, ship without a working `ensurepip` and `venv` by default - users need admin privileges to get things working (see https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/399 ).

Fedora ships a working `ensurepip` and `venv`, but they do so by relying on `pip`'s dependency bundling at the virtual environment level and then keeping the pip package itself up to date.

The Mac OS X system Python is infamously broken by default (in more ways than just not supporting `pip`), as Apple decided to head down the Swift path instead.

`pip` and `setuptools` have the extra problem of actually ending up *in* the run and/or build environments of the projects being developed, giving them an extra incentive to hide their dependencies, whereas `pipenv` is mainly avoiding polluting the user level site-packages directory. However, having a random upgrade in the user site-packages directory break `pipenv` would also be a major problem when it came to keeping `pipenv` managed environments up to date, so there's a significant resilience concern there as well.

So the answer to @venthur's original question that started the issue is "No, it shouldn't, as doing so would make bootstrapping more difficult, and introduce a greater chance of an end user accidentally breaking their ability to update their `pipenv` managed environments in a way they can't readily recover from".

I'm going to close this particular issue on that basis - if folks wanted to open to open separate issues for switching to `pip`-style vendoring (with clear dependency declarations, and automated updates for vendored dependencies), and then configuring pyup.io to monitor those dependencies for new releases, that would be a good thing. 

More generally, I'd ask that folks avoid making the assumption that we aren't already well aware of the risks and trade-offs between fully integrated systems (fewer security updates due to the use of shared components, but  either requiring more coordinated integration testing before an update is released, or else increasing the risks of failure on end user systems due to untested combinations), and isolated application silos (multiple app updates needed for the same security update, but each app can be developed and tested independently without a complex distro-style coordination layer).

The key design assumption that `pipenv` makes that a lot of other developer tools don't is that we expect a large proportion of our user base to be students, educators, and research scientists that are looking to write and run their first Python applications. We default them to running the latest version of everything, and require them to explicitly opt out and pin old versions if that's what they want to do instead, but we mostly assume that they're writing software *for themselves*.

Even for more experienced application developers, we mostly assume that they're either working on deployment to hosted PaaS environments (so dependency updates are just a git push away), app store application development (so they have to bundle anything beyond the base platform APIs regardless), or else they're using `pipenv` to manage the dependencies for their test suite and development environment, rather than for the deployed component.

This *is* going to be irritating for folks that prefer to treat their systems as carefully crafted interlocked webs of dependencies, and don't want to delegate responsibility for any part of that system to anyone else. However, even the traditional Linux operating system developers have conceded that the "giant ball of mud" approach to operating system development breaks down once you reach the level of thousands or tens of thousands of intertwined components, and are switching to technologies like Kubernetes, Flatpak, and Snappy to help break that scaling barrier, while mitigating the harm that can be caused by outdated components in application bundles that haven't updated appropriately.

And everyone that relies on pygments. I have some profiling available at https://github.com/xonsh/import-profiling where I have a nasty `sys.modules['pkg_resources'] = None` hack to prevent its import.

Importing pygments:

* with `pkg_resources`, [244 ms](https://xonsh.github.io/import-profiling/with_pkg_resources_pyg.html)
* without `pkg_resources`, [2.65 ms](https://xonsh.github.io/import-profiling/without_pkg_resources_pyg.html)

So just by importing `pkg_resources`, the slowdown is ~100x.  In wall clock time, I have consistently tested the `pkg_resources` overhead to be at least 150 - 200 ms.  This makes `pkg_resoucres` unusable in command line utilities that require fast start up times.

In xonsh, we have resorted to the above hacks to prevent our dependencies (pygments, prompt_toolkit) from accidentally importing it.

I now understand what is going on, and have successfully reproduced the issue in a test. It also explains why @jaraco, @nakato and I could not reproduce -- it requires the dependencies to be determined from a .dist-info/METADATA file, which uses the problematic format. Just running python setup.py test makes use of install_requires and tests_require, which typically does not use that format, and so everything is fine.

I'm trying to decide if setuptools should support running tests in this model (with pytest-runner) or if the added complication to the test code plus other issues that might arise down the road suggest that this should be an unsupported test runner, and that tests should always be run using tox or with the current setuptools installed into the environment.

Perhaps you have an opinion on the matter? What leads you to use pytest-runner over tox? Would a suitable workaround be to 'setup.py develop' setuptools before running 'ptr'? One of the reasons I've been moving away from pytest-runner is exactly this one, where subprocesses don't get the "package" environment (mainly sys.path) of the parent process without the tests being aware of this contingency. If pytest-runner were the recommended test runner for setuptools, it would certainly be the case that setuptools' test suite should support this mode, but since tox is the recommended runner, I'm inclined not to support the nuances of pytest-runner. I'm open to being convinced if you feel strongly about it.

The problem (as I understand it) is that virtualenv implements its own version of ```site.py``` that doesn't track the core Python version perfectly (that's the issue behind #355, referenced above - if you follow that link, and the link to the various issues linked from there, you'll get the background).

Fixing this isn't as simple as "sync up to the core ```site.py```", because we need to support multiple Python versions, and ```site.py``` has changed between them.

Hope that helps - I'm replying as a virtualenv maintainer offering some guidance, but it's not something I'm planning on working on myself. As usual, patches welcome, but anyone willing to take this on should be clear on the amount of testing effort involved. For example, technically I believe we still support Python 2.6, so do we need to make sure that ```site.getsitepackages()``` is unavailable on that version? (IMO, no, we should just drop support for 2.6, but I wish it were that simple :smile:)

There's a bit of a chain of test runners that are used. At the core is the django.test.runner.DiscoverRunner which uses the unittest.TextTestRunner by default.

However, I have a couple of layers on top of that.

The first layer (working from the inner layer) is xmlrunner.extra.djangotestrunner.XMLTestRunner which overrides run_suite() to run xmlrunner.XMLTestRunner, which in turn overrides unittest.TextTestRunner to produce XML output.

The second and top most layer is a custom override of the build_suite() to exclude specific apps.

Neither of these two layers should alter how a unittest.TestCase is run, unless there's a fundamental issue with unittest.TestCase tests and the decorator.

I've added a keyword argument to `denoise_wavelet` that allows for denoising in different colorspaces. For color images by default this denoises in the YCbCr colorspace.

In doing this, I also allow the noise st.devs to be either a scalar or a list of noise levels in each color plane.

It is still possible to keep the same behavior of plain wavelet thresholding without any conversion to any colorspaces with `colorspace=None`.

Do we want to keep going down this path? It seems like this should be in every denoise function, but it also took me a while to figure how properly denoise an image in YCbCr and have it look good. Maybe a good halfway point is having an example in the documentation of YCbCr, but I'd like for that to be default.
That would be great. Thanks!

Thanks for looking into this and your analysis.

> I think an image of size 9 Mpix is huge for computer vision. Maybe you can scale down your image size?

I am analyzing high-resolution surface image in an industrial application and need the resolution, so this is not an option.

>     18 gradient orientations are quite a lot compared to most other descriptors. You should be able to get decent performance with fewer orientations.

As I am using the descriptors not for matching but as a cheap way to compute texture features, I want to compare different parameter values especially with respect to angular resolution/number of orientations. One assumption for my application is that it would benefit from higher angular resolution and I wanted to validate this.

>     A memory footprint of 13 GB doesn't really seem that big a deal to me for scientific computing applications.

It isn't, and this example runs fine on my machine with 16 GB, but for more orientations it still becomes a problem.

> Therefore, I don't think the implementation of daisy() should be made more memory efficient by processing the input image in chunks as this would increase code complexity. Rather, I think you should subdivide your input image and call daisy() for each image chunk.

Thank you for your conclusion, I will proceed this way and process smaller image regions. I just wanted to know if you saw any other starting points for optimization.

With @blink1073 PR, the rectangle is showed in top left corner:

![skjermbilde 2015-05-07 kl 09 27 14](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4810521/7510602/5c72ea90-f49e-11e4-81d3-c0dbdd48066a.png)

A possible fix is to set rectangle invisible:
```python
    def reset(self):
        self.rect_tool.extents = 0, 0, 0, 0
        self.rect_tool.set_visible(False)
        self.image_viewer.image = self.image_viewer.original_image
        self.image_viewer.ax.relim()
```

But this has the effect that the rectangle corner (blinks in the top corner)[https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/viewer/canvastools/recttool.py#L162-163] before it is hidden.

A possible solution would be setting extents outside the canvas:
```python
    def reset(self):
        self.rect_tool.extents = -10, -10, -10, -10
        self.image_viewer.image = self.image_viewer.original_image
        self.image_viewer.ax.relim()
```

But this feels like a hack. Simply hiding the rectangle is also possible (with the drawback that its still there and can be clicked on, unlikely, but still):
```python
    def reset(self):
        self.rect_tool.set_visible(False)
        self.image_viewer.image = self.image_viewer.original_image
        self.image_viewer.ax.relim()
```

I guess one can also modify `RectangleTool._rect` directly, breaking the `_`-private convention. What do you think?

This is not an issue with python-for-android.

Line endings: different platforms write different line endings. You have to take steps to deal with this, just like you would in any other Python program. A common fix is to use universal newlines ('U' flag to `open()`).

Copyright symbol: this is an encoding issue. ASCII does not have a copyright symbol (that's extended ASCII, which is not what we're talking about). You should decode the text yourself, since Python will assume ASCII. For example, if the file is in UTF-8, you can do something like `fli = file.read().decode('utf8')`. If you don't know the encoding, you can make an educated guess or use something like [chardet](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/chardet), just note that character encoding detection is not reliable.

So, I can confirm that rbit would compile on arm64, but it causes a runtime error. It does compile, the generated asm on an arm64 target looks like:

```
0x1000a8f7c <+1208>: str    w9, [sp, #100]
0x1000a8f80 <+1212>: ldr    w9, [sp, #100]
0x1000a8f84 <+1216>: rbit   x9, x9
0x1000a8f88 <+1220>: str    w9, [sp, #96]

```

For example, you could make this compile after adding my patch by making this change:

```
#if BROTLI_MODERN_COMPILER || defined(__llvm__)
#if defined(BROTLI_TARGET_ARMV7) || defined(BROTLI_TARGET_ARMV8)
static BROTLI_INLINE unsigned BrotliRBit(unsigned input) {
  unsigned output;
  __asm__("rbit %0, %1\n" : "=r"(output) : "r"(input));
  return output;
}
#define BROTLI_RBIT(x) BrotliRBit(x)
#endif  /* armv7 */
#endif  /* gcc || clang */

```

The change I made was to just get BROTLI_TARGET_ARMV7 to only be defined for the ARM 7 targets while keeping it from being defined for arm64 which is v8.

This appears to be almost functional already.  Here are the steps:

1. After importing qgrid and running `nbinstall` at the top of your notebook, add the following line to set some options:
```
qgrid.set_defaults(remote_js=True, show_toolbar=True)
```
2. Render a qgrid and notice the Export button above it.  
3. Click Export to generate a version of the grid that can be rendered outside of the jupyter notebook.  You'll actually see two grids there at this point, and that's OK.
4. Click save to save your notebook.
5. Go to File, Download As, Html to download your notebook as a standalone HTML file.

> but widget outputs are not stored within the notebook file, 

Ah, that makes sense.  There are cases where that's desirable though: notebooks with QGrid outputs can be **much** larger than normal notebooks, because we're rendering thousands of rows of data as in-line javascript object literals.  I imagine that this could be made substantially more efficient by sending the data over a socket in a more appropriate serialization format and then doing the conversion to objects in Javascript.

w/r/t preserving the outputs, I wonder if what this wants is something like an **Embed** or a **Render** button that renders the output statically and writes it as HTML.  That way you could work with the widget view interactively, and when you have an output you want to share/persist, you could hit **Embed** and have the widget view HTML/JS replaced with a static view of the data at the point when you hit the button.

I don't have a great sense of how hard that would be with the current state of the widget system, but it seems like it should work conceptually?

> If we accept changing its function signature, there's no need to have a separate get_clean_factor function.

Originally I meant having a single function both compute the grouping and quantize the factor, returning a data frame containing the original factor. Something like
```Python
quantize_factor(factor, quantiles, groupby, group_labels, ....):
    # compute the grouping, the same code as
    # what is currently in get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns 
    if groupby is not None:
        ......
    
    # compute quantized factor

    # return a merged data frame
    return merged_data
```
The reason why I have this idea is:
1) `quantized_factor` has to know the grouping information. If this information comes from the input `factor_data`, as the current implementation, The user has to create a data frame before calling the function, which can be inconvenient. Alternatively, we can add a `groupby` argument to accept the grouping series/dict. In that case, `quantized_factor` becomes exactly the same as `get_clean_factor `. In either way the grouping info has to come along with the factor, I thought it would be simpler to keep only one function.
2) currently `quantized_factor` returns a `factor_quantile` series. It is not used anywhere other than inside `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`. Is there any use cases where we just want a `factor_quantile` series rather than a `factor_data` data frame?

But now I am hesitant. Keeping `quantized_factor` provides more flexiblity and makes `get_clean_factor` cleaner.



> get_clean_factor_data could be called combine_clean_factor_and_forward_returns maybe (but I am fine with other names)?

`combine_clean_factor_and_forward_returns` is good, consistent with the current API `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`.
Yet `get_factor_data`/`get_clean_factor_data` has its advantage. It indicates its output to be a `factor_data`, which in turn becomes the input of further analysis. I am good with both.



> the data loss can happen in get_clean_factor too, due to the binning

This is another detail I am not sure. Currently, both the data loss in the forward_returns computation and the binning phase are reported in `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`. Should we handle both kind of data loss in `combine_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`? Or handle binning loss in 
handling in `get_clean_factor`, fwd loss in `combine_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`?

That was kind of intended [docs](https://github.com/ansible/tower-cli/blob/master/docs/WORKFLOWS.md):

> The schema command can not delete nodes, so running multiple commands with different schemas will result in creating multiple branches and/or sub-branches.

When looping over the base nodes, it checks to see if a node using JT pk=13 exists. Apparently not finding such a node in that workflow, it creates a new one and carries on.

To do what you expected, delete the workflow, create a new one, and run the schema command. We could certainly have a flag like `--destroy-existing` that does this same thing. I don't want to get into the business of hand-picking ones to delete. If the user wants to delete a node, they should somehow go about starting over.

Hi @jamessewell,

this is expected behavior, Patroni always starts postgres in recovery (with recovery.conf), no matter which DCS is used.

There are set of actions needs to be done before promote:
1. Check that you are still allowed to be a master (tags.nofailover == false)
2. Check that your wal position is not far behind the last registered in DCS (pg_current_xlog_location > /optime/leader - maximum_lag_on_failover)
3. Compare your wal position against all other nodes and verify that you not behind any of the others
4. Take leader lock
5. And only if all previous checks succeeded - promote

Without all this checks you can't know that you are allowed to become the master. And actually how do you know that you have need shut down as master? Absence of recovery.conf? It doesn't really work.
If there is no consensus available, Patroni will always create recovery.conf and restart postgres in read-only.

I think timeline bump is not really a big problem, in reality it happens very rare, that your master is dying unexpectedly.
