Got a working solution.

Note for datacats environments using ckanext-spatial:
* Create environment, let datacats initiate db as per normal
* datacats tweak --install-postgis
* do not db clean / db init

## Migrate users
* Dump the user table (data only, no schema) from the source CKAN
* Chown the sql file to yourself and an appropiate group (e.g. www-data)
* Delete the first three users from user.sql - the new CKAN will already have the "default" user.
```
sudo su
sudo -u postgres pg_dump -a -O -t user -f path/to/user.sql CKAN_DB_NAME
sudo chown florianm:www-data /mnt/projects/datacats/internal/user.sql
# delete lines "default", "logged_in", "visitor" from user.sql
```

## Restore users in target CKAN
* In this example, the target is a datacats CKAN environment.
* Datacats CKAN: print out the auto-generated passwords and copy the "postgres" one

```
cat ~/.datacats/ENV_NAME/sites/primary/passwords.ini
# prints passwords, copy postgres passwird
(datacats venv)me@machine:/path/to/ENV_NAME⟫ datacats shell
# datacats shell:
(ckan)shell@ENV_NAME:~$ psql -h db -d ckan -U postgres -f /path/to/user.sql
exit
```

## Backup groups, orgs, datasets
* Has to run after migrating users
* Source CKAN has to be online
```
ckanapi dump groups --all -p 3 -r http://internal-data.dpaw.wa.gov.au > path/to/groups.jsonl
ckanapi dump organizations --all -p 3 -r http://internal-data.dpaw.wa.gov.au > path/to/organizations.jsonl
ckanapi dump datasets --all -p 6 -r http://internal-data.dpaw.wa.gov.au > path/to/datasets.jsonl
```

## Load groups, orgs, datasets
* -I is capital i = load jsonl data
* Target CKAN has to be online
```
ckanapi load groups -p 3 -I path/to/groups.jsonl -r http://TARGET -a APIKEY
ckanapi load organizations -p 3 -I path/to/organizations.jsonl -r http://TARGET -a APIKEY
ckanapi load datasets -p 3 -I path/to/datasets.jsonl -r http://TARGET -a APIKEY
```
Where APIKEY is admin to TARGET CKAN.

## Migrate files, fix permissions
* This example migrates between two datacats CKANs. Other CKANs have to adjust their paths.
```
sudo rsync -Pavvr ~/.datacats/SOURCE/sites/primary/files/ ~/.datacats/TARGET/sites/primary/files/
sudo chown -R florianm:www-data ~/.datacats/TARGETites/primary/files/ 
```

## Download, install, enable extensions

## 1. New formula syntax

Let's start with an example:

Before:

``` py
class revenu_disponible(Variable):
    column = FloatCol
    entity_class = Individus
    label = u"Revenu disponible de l'individu"

    def function(self, simulation, period):
        period = period.start.period(u'year').offset('first-of')
        rsa = simulation.calculate_add('rsa', period)
        salaire_imposable = simulation.calculate('salaire_imposable', period)
        taux = simulation.legislation_at(period.start).impot.taux

        return period, rsa + salaire_imposable * (1 - taux)
```

After:

``` py
class revenu_disponible(Variable):
    column = FloatCol
    entity = Individu
    label = u"Revenu disponible de l'individu"

    def function(individu, period, legislation):
        period = period.start.period(u'year').offset('first-of')
        rsa = individu('rsa', period, options = [ADD])
        salaire_imposable = individu('salaire_imposable', period)
        taux = legislation(period).impot.taux

        return period, rsa + salaire_imposable * (1 - taux)
```
#### Changes
- The object simulation is not the main object the formula writer uses. Instead, everything is about the *object* of your calculation, for instance a person, or a family. As $laem first mentioned, calculating the `salary` of a person seems more natural, in the context of writing formulas, than calculating the `salary` of the simulation. The simulation object, who is more about the simulation execution than the formula definition, is not a concept the formula writer needs to care about.

- We use `individu` singular, as a formula is conceptually written for **a** person. The underlying object is of course vectorial, but this is an implementation problem that the formula writer should have to worry about the least as possible.

- The `legislation` is now the (optional) third argument of a formula. It is a function that can be called with a period (or an instant) for argument, and will compute the legislation at `period.start`.
-  We don't use `calculate_add` and equivalents. We discussed with $michelbl that `add` and `divide` would better be just an option of the calculation, and not a fully different method.
- We rename `entity_class` to `entity`: the formula writer just needs to mention what is the object of his.her formula, the concept of class is not needed. <sup><sub>This modification is currently reverted to reduce france changelog, but will be re-added.</sub><sup>

- We don't explicitly use a `calculate` method, but the `person('salary', period)` notation instead. It is less heavy than the current syntax, and it reduces the cognitive load when reading a formula.

This line is very confusing to me.

This is how I'd write the formula without any language constraint (pseudo code): 


```yaml
Variable xxx
    domain: Famille

    formula:
        and:
            Famille.eligibilite
            sum(Famille.individus.salaire) < plafond
```
We might even go further and not specify `Famille.` since it is obvious in the context of this variable. 

The little bit of magic here is the part `individus.salaire` because here '.salaire' is sugar for 'individus.map(individu => individu.salaire)'. I'm not sure the python style should be replaced by a functional one. I'm in favor of a very natural syntax for these types of operations on entities.

Going even further, accessing a property of a list could implicitly return the sum of the properties of each item of the list when the comparison is done against a scalar, here `plafond` :

```yaml
Variable xxx
    domain: Famille

    formula:
        and:
            eligibilite
            individus.salaire < plafond
```

The domain Entity is this only way to access variables (hence we omit it here). They are unambiguous (always namespaced by their entity).

Problem is: https://github.com/aws/chalice/blob/master/chalice/cli/factory.py#L122

So `load_chalice_app` gets called twice, the first time is inside of `create_config_obj` where it gets set: `user_provided_params['chalice_app'] = app_obj` which kind of seems pointless since the only thing that depends on this is the route verification... 

When I remove the two lines and change `run_local_server` to:

```python
def run_local_server(factory, host, port, stage, env):
    # type: (CLIFactory, str, int, str, MutableMapping) -> None
    config = factory.create_config_obj(
        chalice_stage_name=stage
    )
    # We only load the chalice app after loading the config
    # so we can set any env vars needed before importing the
    # app.
    env.update(config.environment_variables)
    print('run_local_server: ' + os.getenv('__FOO__', 'None'))
    app_obj = factory.load_chalice_app()
    # Check that `chalice deploy` would let us deploy these routes, otherwise
    # there is no point in testing locally.
    routes = app_obj.routes
    validate_routes(routes)
    # When running `chalice local`, a stdout logger is configured
    # so you'll see the same stdout logging as you would when
    # running in lambda.  This is configuring the root logger.
    # The app-specific logger (app.log) will still continue
    # to work.
    logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stdout)
    server = factory.create_local_server(app_obj, config, host, port)
    server.serve_forever()
```

Things seem to work fine now, so environment variables can be accessed in `app.py` anywhere.

I've gone about implementing integration tests for my Chalice apps by creating an instance of the `LocalGateway` and using that to implement my tests. The local gateway is the same handler that Chalice uses to create the event object when running locally.

Below is a small example:
```python
import json
from unittest import TestCase

from chalice.config import Config
from chalice.local import LocalGateway

from app import app


class TestApp(TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        self.lg = LocalGateway(app, Config())

    def test_get_books(self):
        response = self.lg.handle_request(method='GET',
                                          path='/books',
                                          headers={},
                                          body='')

        assert response['statusCode'] == 200
        assert json.loads(response['body']) == [
            dict(book_id=1),
            dict(book_id=2)
        ]

    def test_post_book(self):
        body = dict(book_info='hello')
        response = self.lg.handle_request(method='POST',
                                          path='/books',
                                          headers={
                                              'Content-Type': 'application/json'
                                          },
                                          body=json.dumps(body))

        assert response['statusCode'] == 201
        assert response['headers'].get('Location') == '/books/3'
```

More thorough examples can be found in a repo that I created [here](https://github.com/nplutt/chalice-test-examples#unit-tests).

I can't run your code, and that's not a minimal working example... so I'm hindered in helping you unless I can run the code.  The typical thing to do is to post a minimal sample of code that reproduces the error. 

What I can say from your snippet above is that `masterrun` is going to make serialization harder.  First, the variables in the function are not encapsulated, so it a lot of global references.  That's not a good idea -- it works best if you either pass or import all variables you need.  Second, the function is defined above the `pool`.  If you are going to do weird stuff like use global references, then it's often better to import the function.

Ron,

Honestly, I don't know the answer to that.   However, it's a very "unusual" use case to profile the code in parallel.  Typically, you profile in another node, and in the same workflow as your code without profiling -- but the intent is to not profile in parallel beyond the `Pool` with one node (which is one reason there's a sharp limitation on the number of processes that the profiling `Pool` can take in `pipe`).  Either I never tried it, or more likely, I put in the limitation on the pool to one process because I tried something similar to what you are and found that profiling doesn't work with more than one process/thread -- but I don't remember.

I would however, caution you to be careful that you are using two different `Pool`.  Typically if you have a hierarchy of `Pool`, you should use one `ThreadPool` and one `ProcessPool`, because `pathos` does some things behind the scenes to reuse pools so it removes the overhead of creating them repeatedly.  What I mean by that is, if you use `pathos.pools.ProcessingPool` and `pathos.multiprocessing.Pool`, you may actually be using the same `Pool` behind the scenes and not know it.  The intent is to use the interface in `pathos.pools`, the other is deprecated.

```python
for t, v in ts:

You can do something like `python plot.py | xmgrace -` to redirect the output of the script into xmgrace, adjust the visual formatting as necessary in the GUI, and ultimately output the images as SVG.

there is logic in: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/ba58a854d561dcf6963c0097cad544bfe2861c5a/pex/util.py#L167

it might be worth noting that the `.pexrc` feature can inject environment variables for you if your environment doesn't support that. Placing a `.pexrc` file in the same CWD (or in $HOME) the pex file will read it.

Another way of achieving what you want without modifying pex source is to simply have a wrapper script that handles this `sys.argv` inspection and then runs an `os.execve` with environment variables set. Or even just a bash script, here's an example of one a tool I use builds:

```
#!/bin/bash

exec /usr/bin/env PEX_ROOT="$BASEDIR/libexec" PEX_MODULE="apollo:main" $BASEDIR/bin/server.pex "$@"
```

It should also be noted that you can use pex files as the shebang of a script a la `#!/usr/bin/env yourpex.pex`. 

Not trying to discourage this change, just offering potential solutions that might work w/o cutting a new feature.

Coverage increased (+0.09%) to 66.7% when pulling **80492f6a02ce52a1dc68843dab2476b6106689d4 on jsirois:jsirois/python_chroot/compile** into **4691897cc21d1a7863fb28374ae7e3cb1de1b73c on pantsbuild:master**.

This issue presents a good excuse to finally (re)implement @wickman's work from long ago  introducing a product pipeline centered on PEX chroot products.  Roughly:
```
resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot -> compile.python-eval(PythonChroot):None
resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot -> test.pytest(PythonChroot):None
resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot -> repl.py(PythonChroot):None
resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot -> run.py(PythonChroot):None
resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot ->  binary.python-binary-create(PythonChroot):None
resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot ->  setup-py(PythonChroot):None
``` 

Where the representation is `<task>(<product deps>*):<product>`.

This though still leaves test not depending on compile, so either the new `python-chroot` task would need to be installed in the `compile` goal or else a compile-product would need to be introduced for {test.pytest, reply.py, run.py, binary.python-binary-create, setup-py} to depend on - say `CompiledPythonChroot`.  In fact though the current `PythonChroot` ~product offered up via `PythonTask` superclass methods is compiled (`.pyc` are generated) since all chroots, cached and temporary, `freeze` their PexBuilder.

So I'm thinking introducing `compile.python-chroot():PythonChroot` instead of `resolve.python-chroot():PythonChroot` is the way to go here.  Since pex currently mixes resolution and assembly / compilation this doesn't even stray from the current underlying model.

from tornado.wsgi import WSGIContainer
from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop

from prometheus_client import start_http_server, Summary

from flask import Flask
from prometheus_client import Counter

app = Flask(__name__)

REQUEST_TIME = Summary('request_processing_seconds','Time Spent processing request')

@app.route('/')
@REQUEST_TIME.time()
def handler():
    return "hello world"

class MainHandler(RequestHandler):
    def get(self):
        self.write("This message comes from Tornado")

tr = WSGIContainer(app)

application = Application([
                          (r"/tornado", MainHandler),
                          (r".*", FallbackHandler, dict(fallback=tr)),
                          ])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    application.listen(5000)
    start_http_server(9999)
    IOLoop.instance().start()

@thedangler I think there is often some misconception about when to use uris and when not to, because we have support for them. But for any normal soco functionality, you really do not need to. Sonos playlists are not really meant to be played as a stream (as you seemed to be trying to do, that is only for radios and others such stream to be played in single play mode without affecting the queue), they are meant to be added to the queue and player from there. When you do that, this whole thing becomes much less magical:

```python
>>> # Provided z is my sonos zone
... 
>>> print(z)
<SoCo object at ip 192.168.0.30>
>>> first_sonos_playlist = z.get_sonos_playlists()[0]
>>> # Asking for sonos playlists, returns to you a SearchResult, which can be iterated over and indexed like a list, so we pull out the first item
...
>>> print(first_sonos_playlist)
<DidlPlaylistContainer 'b'?h Abe'' at 0x7f9105182668>
>>> # So asking for playlists, actually returns playlists, which can be added to the queue
... 
>>> z.get_queue()
Queue(items=[])
>>> # Ok, so it is empty now
... 
>>> z.add_to_queue(first_sonos_playlist)
1
>>> z.get_queue()

```
sonosDevice = SoCo("IP-ADDESS")

# Subscribe to receive notifications of changes
sub = sonosDevice.avTransport.subscribe()

keepLooping = True
queueItem = None
while keepLooping:
        try:
            # Get the most recent event received
            while not sub.events.empty():
                try:
                    # Get the next event - but do not block or wait for an event
                    # if there is not already one there
                    queueItem = sub.events.get(False)
                except:
                    log("Sonos: Queue get failed: %s" % traceback.format_exc())

            # Now log the details of an event if there is one there
            if queueItem is not None:
                log("Event details: %s" % queueItem)
        except:
            log("Sonos: Failed to get latest event details: %s" % traceback.format_exc())

	if queueItem is not None:
		keepLooping = False
```

> **lawrenceakka** [commented on issue 428](https://github.com/SoCo/SoCo/issues/428#issuecomment-289300915):
> Sonos has changed the way in which music service account data can be accessed. It used to be possible to get the necessary info from the players, but it is now encrypted using AES-CBC. Although other third party Sonos controllers have worked out how to get round this and decrypt the info (see here for a discussion), I am not sure that it would be right for us to do so in an open source project (though I haven't decided yet).

We should probably defer to the CLI for interaction patterns when it makes sense.  In this case, @gnuoy's option 3 most closely matches the CLI, so we could change the signature to:

```python
    async def set_config(self, config=None, to_default=None):
        """
        Set application config to specific values, or reset to default values.

        :param dict config: A mapping of config options to values to be set to.
            If not given, no options will be set.
        :param list to_default: A list of config options to be reset to their default value.
            If not given, no options will be reset.
        """
```

(The flag for the CLI is `--reset`, so we could also rename it, but `to_default` is perhaps a bit more descriptive in this case, a la `app.set_config(to_default=['opt1', 'opt2'])`.)

OTOH, it might be more Pythonic to split the method into two methods that each do one thing: `set_config(dict)` and `reset_config(list)`.  I think this is what I was trying to suggest with my previous comment, as I think that `ApplicationFacade.Unset` would in fact reset to default without having to manually query the defaults, but that would need to be tested.  Certainly, `app.reset_config(list)` is a better method name than `app.unset_config(list)`.

Anyway, I'm fine with either approach.

Yes, the binary GPOS interpolation is ok. What I mean is that we should try to avoid calling fontmake twice, once to build the master fonts with `--mti-source` option, and then again for the instances with `-i --interpolate-binary-layout` options. Not only this looks a bit confusing to me, but we are also creating the master UFOs twice, the first time to build the master TTFs, the second time to generate the (interpolated?) instance UFOs from which the binary instances are built.

I'm thinking that we should perhaps change it so that any of these scenarios are supported:

1) MTI sources exist only for the masters and one wishes to only build "master" OTFs or TTFs; instances correspond one to one to the masters, and are there only to make Glyphs.app happy (e.g. the Noto Urdu Nastaliq has two masters which are deliberately not interpolatable; see https://github.com/googlei18n/noto-source/pull/89):
```
fontmake -g Font.glyphs --mti-source Font.plist
```

2) MTI sources exist for all the instances and no binary GPOS interpolation is thus required
```
fontmake -g Font.glyphs --interpolate --mti-source Font.plist
```

3) if MTI sources exist only for the masters (or exists for some but not all the instances?) and binary layout interpolation is needed for the instances (that don't already have layout tables)
```
fontmake -g Font.glyphs -i --mti-source Font.plist --interpolate-binary-layout
```

(1) is already supported, (2) is the subject of the current PR, and (3) is the situation that currently requires a two-step approach.

Related issue: #326, #370

> SMC server periodically (at least once per block) triggers the ShardTracker for new logs

Some thoughts about the roles:
1. A node may use `NotaryService` and `ProposerService` and the same time. So I think the trigger point of calling `ShardTracker.get_add_header_logs()` should be only in one place that shared by the `SMCService`s?
2. A node may watch multiple shards, and the notary pool info from SMC should be shared cross services.

Another direction is  that in the earlier draft design, there is a new `ShardMonitor` **process** that is responsible to poll SMC and notify `ShardManager` that manages multiple shards. `ShardManager` is just a very abstract idea by that time, it can be considered as the middle layer between `ShardServer` and `ShardSyncer`. The sub-services (`NotaryService`, `ProposerService`) of different shards can have a pubsub relationship with `ShardMonitor`.

#### Pros:
1. Making the polling service aside by the server, clearer roles.
2. May be easier to share the same data from SMC?

#### Cons:
1. Neet to maintain a new process alive well.
2. The performance cost of maintaining IPC?

I'd like to migrate to conda from pip as to not deal with the scientific python stack baggage (blas, etc., when building from source on linux) as well as the reap the performance benefits associated with the recent mkl redistribution. However, I'd also like to maintain the cross-platform nature that pip provides e.g., between Mac and Linux.

There are two problems, as far as I can tell.

1.) Generating an environment.yml ("conda env export") or spec-file.txt ("conda list -e") lists the version dependency as well as the specific build. The build component is not cross-platform (might exist in one os channel but not the other) :
  numpy=1.10.4=py27_1
  version: 1.10.4
  build: py27_0

2.) Non-python components are not cross-platform. e.g., scipy on Linux depends on libgfortran, but scipy on Mac does not.

It has been pointed out elsewhere that hand-editing the environment file can create something that gets auto-resolved when a user does 'conda install ...' (https://www.continuum.io/blog/developer-blog/explicit-conda-environment-specifications#comment-2368808739) but I would really be interested in an intelligent environment export function that does this automatically.

This was discussed in #864<https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/864>, with the answer being i.e. "no_softlinks should be used more judiciously in package recipes." Unfortunately, the reality is that any executable that has dynamic library dependencies in conda is not symlink-suitable, and since symlinks really only make sense cross-filesystem, hard links are really the only suitable solution.

As a result, I suspect conda is not generally usable across filesystems for many packages unless the --copy argument to conda create is used (which is, unfortunately, slow). A (e.g.) --softlink-except-executables (that would be the default if the package and env filesystem are not the same) would make sense for this case and be slightly faster than --copy.

@kalefranz although I rarely use conda-build and I've not used it so far for setting up development environments, to me it feels a bit hacky to use a "tool for *building* conda packages" to actually just *install* packages in a new development environment. In my case -- create and submit PRs -- I don't need a recipe to build a conda package. Using `conda install package; conda remove package` would be easier than downloading or writing a recipe.

I actually like @jhprinz's suggestion of having a `--add-dependencies-from-package PACKAGE` option (maybe with a more succinct name like `--deps-from PACKAGE` ?). I can update #3500 to implement that option if needed.

As for whether RStudio is installed into your R environment, I am not sure, but if it were, then I do not think you would have run into this issue. I think the forthcoming `Anaconda Navigator` release may contain some changes around this behaviour though? @goanpeca will know better.
I have noticed that conda rebuilds the PATH environment variable using a semicolon `;` (Windows style) instead of the colon `:` (POSIX style), completely breaking the search path in msys2.

@FrancescAlted Thanks for taking the time to respond. I appreciate the desire to keep things simple and maintainable.

**Categorical dtype issue:**
As you suggested I currently use compression to deal with low cardinality string data. This works extremely well from a storage stand-point. Where it breaks down is in the interaction with pandas. Pandas does not really do strings. I thus now have two choices when interfacing with pandas:
 1. I factorise the string columns every single time I read from the ctable, which keeps complexity low but absolutely kills performance, or 
 2. I use bquery factorization, avoid ctable `__getitem__()` altogether for reading the data and load the factorization manually. This leads to messy code and is wasteful on storage since I am carrying around the original data + the factor + the "index?"/"key?".

In short: to me categoricals as relating to bcolz are only really an issue with strings. Compression will take case of the other dtypes in my use cases. Maybe limiting categoricals to strings would change the complexity vs. utility analysis. I currently do not have a clear enough idea on how categoricals would be implemented to asses this.

**Row-major ordering issue:** 
A dictionary of numpy arrays does indeed solve the row-major ordering issue. I did some tests before I discovered pandas already uses column-major order. I did not propose it because it would would change the data access model and I had gotten the impression that that was a big no-go with bcolz:

Currently projection to a subset of the columns is identical with numpy and ctable: `mydata[['col1', 'col2']]` does the trick. If ctable were to return a dict of numpy arrays, this would no longer be true:

```
In [9]: test = {'col1': [1, 2, 3], 'col2': [4, 5, 6], 'col3': [7, 8, 9]}

In [10]: test[['col1', 'col2']]
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-10-ac76cd049698> in <module>()
----> 1 test[['col1', 'col2']]

TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
```

While a pandas out_flavour would preserve the api:
```
In [32]: test = {'col1': [1, 2, 3], 'col2': [4, 5, 6], 'col3': [7, 8, 9]}

In [33]: test1 = pd.DataFrame(data=test)

In [34]: test1[['col1', 'col2']]
Out[34]:
   col1  col2
0     1     4
1     2     5
2     3     6
```

If that break in the api is not an issue, a dict of arrays would be great for pandas: the built-in functions can ingest them fairly cheaply.

"Fairly" cheaply because:
 1. it has the DataFrame instantiation overhead (which ctable could probably cache), and 
 2. it copies the arrays into a newly created array (pandas collates columns with identical dtypes into something it calls "blocks").
   - The copying however will be efficient as it will copying contiguous data.
   - Of course direct decompression into a pre-allocated DataFrame would be even faster but that is the complexity vs. maintainability tradeoff again. I cannot imagine the gains would be worth the effort: but profiling keeps surprising me...

So I'm looking at your `drv_elastic` service, a couple of notes:

1. you are using your own build of ES. So I don't know what exactly is going on with your custom ES image. This could or could not be an issue. There's just not enough info to tell

@brendandburns, the `test_acs_create_update` was re-recorded to ensure non kubernetes acs was created correctly. I also manually ran the command to create a kubernetes acs. I can capture that scenario to a new test if you can tell me the property list to verify.

I want to make sure that the design works nicely with future managed failover group and job target group commands. Specifically

- What will be the planned syntax for managed failover group? (Managed failover group will be able to contain both MI and servers/db, right?)
- `failover-group add-databases --databases x y z` a few comments:
  - I think adding/removing should be part of `failover-group update`. e.g. `failover-group update --add-db  x y z`. This allows multiple properties to be set in one go. 
  - `failover-group create` also should have the ability to add db's
- `--partner-server-name` -> `--partner-server`
- `--partner-resource-group-name` -> `--partner-resource-group`
- Please comment what happens if `--partner-resource-group` is not specified
- `--allow-read-only-failover-to-primary: (...) This feature is not yet supported.` - so why is there a param for it? :)
- `--failover-policy` - what are allowed values? Can you put `enum` in params.py?
- `failover`
  - I know "failover" is in the name of the resource, but please consider `set-primary` in order to match same as `az sql db replica set-primary` command (single db geodr). 
  - please make sure it is idempotent.
- `--grace-period-with-data-loss` this is very long param name. We are not implementing powershell here ;) can it just be `--grace-period` ?

I completely understand that, but .msi certainly is not the only installation technology.
In my humble opinion, it is increasingly difficult to install Azure CLI in a Windows Container and the entire nature of a Nano Container becomes a no-go because of a limited range of installation options on Windows. Why is it that you have Bash installation scripts, yum, apt-get, pip options for Linux, but only an MSI installer package for Windows? 
Certainly I didn't open this issue to learn that MSI is not supported on Windows Server Nano, but because I want Azure CLI on Windows Server Nano.
Would it be an idea to have an installer for Windows which copies to right files and adds the URI to the PATH environment variable instead of relying only on MSI packages?

I'm still -1 on supporting multiple checkpoints in the default manager. Then we are just reinventing VCS badly. Alternative checkpoint implementations can wrap git or database systems to provide it, but I strongly oppose implementing mini-git on the filesystem.

I don't have a good answer for accidental deletion. Failing to delete the checkpoints on deletion of notebooks means that we are guaranteeing that we clutter the filesystem with hidden files with no intention of or mechanism for deleting them, which is no good. On the other hand, we could add to the contents manager our own version of trash, which is reimplementing yet another wheel. If we do this, I would propose that we do our best to actually call system APIs to put things in the system trash, rather than implementing our own trash folder.

> Has there been any discussion around infinite scrolling for output areas

Yes, and more generally about the notebook itself. 
THe issue is with widgets, or other elements that attach events to the DOM. You can't not display (or remove) them as it will fail to register some events. If it's a "safe" mime then that would be fine. 

Another question would be how to "recycle" things that are high on the page. 
That _could_ be done, by replacing elements with fixed height divs (measuring just after things get out of sight far enough). Annoying if windows resize but doable. 

In the end it would be easier if model was on server side and do lazy loading. But that's far away. 

Beyond infinite scrolling, we can just collapse something to be and ask a "would you like to display everything".

Sorry about this disruption. Allow me to explain a bit why we've made this change.

Issue #1830 pointed out that an attacker running a malicious DNS server can get round the same-origin requirements which browsers normally impose. It seems likely that this would apply to Jupyter running in its default configuration on localhost. If so, the practical upshot of this would be that a malicious website could use your browser to communicate with Jupyter and run code on your system.

This reminded us of a less severe problem that we've been aware of for a while: on a system with multiple users logged in, any user can access any other user's notebook server which is not protected by a password.

Neither of these should be a problem if you're running in Jupyterhub, or if you have configured the notebook with a password. But many people (myself included) use the notebook in its default configuration: listening on localhost with no password, trusting that either we're the only one logged in to the machine, or we can trust anyone else who may log in.

So the token mechanism is intended to restrict access to the server while inconveniencing the standard user as little as possible. When you start the server, it generates two tokens: one reusable, one one-time. The reusable one is shown in the terminal (or log) to use when connecting another browser. The one-time token is used to open the notebook in the primary browser - opening a URL involves passing it as a command line argument, which on some platforms is visible to other users, so we don't pass the reusable token. When the browser provides either token in the URL, the server gives it a cookie to authenticate future requests.

Attackers using the DNS trick, and other users sharing the system with you, should not know either an auth cookie nor a usable auth token, so the server will deny their requests.

We know a change like this is going to cause some pain, but we think it's necessary to fix a rather nasty security problem. We'll do our best to help you figure out how to make it work with your use cases.

@viaregio The token is randomly generated each time you start the server, so there isn't really a fixed default. Unfortunately the autogenerated config docs don't know about that.

I think there's a reasonable case where you're invoking a set of tasks asynchronously for their side effects (e.g. downloading a thousand small files to a directory on disk), and they don't have a meaningful return value. I.e. something you'd do synchronously like:

```python
for i in range(1000):
    do_thing(i)
```

Thinking about it a bit more, I think the answer may be to use [asyncio.gather](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.gather) in place of `asyncio.wait()` - that should immediately propagate an exception if one is raised.

Thanks @kunalbhutani. Just to check, the "Character" type means the expected value is a single character, whereas the "String" type means a variable number of characters, is that right? Sounds like it should be obvious but it's not explicitly stated in the spec.

Yes, you're right about the funny behavior of default values. The import process always converts a `None` into the default if there is one. That doesn't make sense and will be sorted out. 

As for processing partial instances, the goal is to be able to make a distinction between
  * an explicit `None`
  * a value that has not been set.

Currently these end up being the same thing, which makes it impossible to do PATCH etc.

And it's not just about parsing incoming data. Serializing instances while leaving out unset fields must also be possible. Consequently, validating the data needs to be possible as well.

The current strategy (which certainly is up for discussion) is to simply enable missing values on instances. It's not about enabling some magical "alternative mode" but to allow an absence of values. (Traditionally, there's been a guarantee that all fields have *some* value — at least a `None`. All I'm doing is getting rid of this guarantee.)

If your application does not need to distinguish between `None` and "not set", things won't change much if at all. The compatibility issue only has to do with the import/instantiation options. See, in the current version instantiating a model

  * populates all fields with `None`
  * sets `partial=True`.

I wanted to repurpose the `partial` parameter to simultaneously act as a shortcut for a setup that makes sense for *partial instances*, but it's impossible to fully reconcile the above combination with that.

That's all this is, really.

I've gotten the branch to a point where all tests pass with minimal changes to the tests themselves. Here's how it looks:

```python
class M(Model):
    a = IntType()
    b = IntType()
```
```python
>>> m = M({'a': 1})
>>> m.a
1
>>> m.b # None
>>> m._data
{'a': 1, 'b': None}
```

See? By default it works *exactly* like now, except it wouldn't enforce `required` on the import step because I've used `partial=False` to achieve the automatic population with `None`s.

In contrast, setting `partial=True` implies `init_values=False`, and the missing field `b` won't get autofilled:

```python
>>> m = M({'a': 1}, partial=True)
>>> m.a
1
>>> m.b
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
schematics.exceptions.MissingValueError
>>> m._data
{'a': 1, 'b': Undefined}
>>> 
```

As shown above, trying to access a field that's not set will raise an exception as per normal Python behavior. The `MissingValueError` is a subclass of `AttributeError`, so you don't have to look for it specifically.

Internally, missing values are represented by `Undefined` placeholder objects, which preserves parity between `_data` and `_fields` and makes it easy to work with the internals since you can just iterate over everything.

In summary, the whole partial object stuff in my view is not so much about representing two different things than simply extending the capabilities of model instances. There are no two kinds of instances. You can instantiate a "regular" object and turn it into a "partial" object by saying `del obj.field`.

Turns out, given our work on STACKs, we don't have space for a consensus hash in `registration` or `renewal`.  That's probably fine, however:

* should two or more fork-sets emerge, the only way a `registration` could be valid in more than one is if its `preorder` is also valid in more than one (i.e. the fork-sets emerged shortly after a `preorder`).

* a `renewal` is valid in all fork-sets where the name has the same owner.  If a user does not want their name to be renewed by a separate fork-set (unlikely, because it's free for them), they can `transfer` or `update` their name to force it to exist in a single fork-set.

Hi @ddurham2, apologies for the delay on answering this. I missed the issue notification and have been busy on other projects.

The README is correct regarding 1.2 support, however we do have a few minor 1.3 / 1.4 features supported as well (e.g., attributes, object arguments). We don't have any major 1.3 / 1.4 features supported just yet (e.g., operations).

The long term plan for PyKMIP is to fully support every feature of the KMIP specification, from 1.0 to the latest version, now 1.4. The focus lately has been on adding in 1.2 cryptographic operations, like encrypting/decrypting/signing data and wrapping/deriving encryption keys. We'll be cutting the next release, 0.7, within the next few weeks and then after that I'll continue working on outstanding features and pull requests.

In general, I have a long list of features covering the different KMIP versions that I'm slowly working my way through. However, if you have a specific feature that you are interested in, definitely file an issue and I'll bump it up my priority queue.

Regarding client/server profiles, it's really just a matter of adding in the underlying features that make up a given profile. Once those features are supported, the profile is supported. My intent is to eventually support all profiles. Additionally, official support for the different KMIP profiles was better codified in 1.3 and 1.4 (i.e., official enumerations now exist for each recognized profile). Before the profiles and their names/definitions would kind of change from version to version. Now that a standardized list exists, it should be easier to break down support and map out development for each one.

This just shoes that your login shell has it set. Tells us nothing about upstart or mesos-slave.

Upstart is Ubuntu's init system. Google "upstart ubuntu" to find out more. TOIL_WORKDIR needs to be set in the Toil worker process which is started by the Toil/Mesos executor, which is started by the mesos-slave, which is started by upstart. If upstart doesn't read /etc/environment, TOIL_WORKDIR won't be set in any of these processes. Toil jobs run in the worker process. If you can see observe TOIL_WORKDIR in a Toil job, you have shown that it is present in the worker and executor processes where it is used.

The most sustainable method of verifying that is as part of the tests in the cgcloud-toil distribution.

I think this is a bug in CoreRCON. It assumes that [its `TCPPacketReceived` method](https://github.com/ScottKaye/CoreRCON/blob/0686557/src/CoreRCON/RCON.cs#L131) will be called for every "packet". But TCP is a stream-based protocol, it does not really have packets (or rather, it uses packets only as an implementation detail), you can't assume that the buffer you receive contains exactly the information you want, not more or less, as is explained in [the documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.net.sockets.socket.receiveasync?view=netcore-1.1#remarks):

> For byte stream-style sockets, incoming data is placed into the buffer until the buffer is filled, the connection is closed, or the internally buffered data is exhausted.

If it seems to work reliably on Windows, then that's probably just luck.

And CoreRCON is also the reason why the exception is uncatchable: it's thrown from `TCPPacketReceived`, which is the event handler for `SocketAsyncEventArgs.Completed`. As far as I know, if the handler throws an exception (and doesn't handle it itself), you can't catch it.

Here is another idea. 

Message `SeqId` can be viewed as a mutation number. A topic has a set of messages. When the set is mutated (changed) by adding a message to it (someone sent `{pub}`), the `SeqId` is incremented by 1. Because messages are sent one by one, every message can be assigned a unique ID which is also a mutation index.

What if I do the same with deletions? Deletion is also a mutation after all. In addition to `SeqID` I will create another Id, say `DelId`. Any time someone soft- or hard-deletes messages the `DelId` is incremented by 1 and the `delId` is assigned to the deleted messages. Then I'll add `what="del"` (in addition to "desc", "sub" and "data") to `{sub}` and `{get}` messages and will report `DelId` to users. The server will report the list of deleted message IDs in another `{meta}` message.

I think this is a cleaner and better approach than dealing with `UpdatedAt`.

These are all extremely recent accessions. In fact, 4 of 5 are .1 accessions that were officially released *after* the July 7 data dump that was used to build UTA. (I don't understand how records released in late July and Aug would appear on downloads on July 7, but that is indeed what happened.) As a result, NCBI apparently didn't have NM-NP associations for these, and thus they're absent from UTA.

hgvs translates the cdna into a protein sequence. A table of NM-NP pairs is used to find the corresponding protein sequence accession. If none exists, the MD5 of the sequence is used as a pseudo accession.

I suspect this issue will go away after the next UTA build. I'm going to close this for now. Please reopen if it's not fixed after the next UTA build.

@ppolewicz: A `sync` command or similar is baked into the command-line tools for every other cloud file storage. For a good reason, since it is the most practical way to upload a larger set of files. See:

- https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil/commands/rsync
- Azure file storage uses SMB, so standard `rsync` works

Also, don't mixup sync with backup. Being used by a backup implementation isn't the same as being one. This is similar to `b2 ls` in that is attempts to provide a usable command-line abstraction for the raw API. Which, after all, is the purpose of this tool.

Related to #111.

The semantics I'm used to for non-blocking or timeout write() is that if any bytes at all are written successfully, the write() call returns with the number of bytes written. If *no* bytes can be written before the timeout expires, that can either be handled by throwing an exception, or by returning zero.

(Returning zero is far more efficient; exceptions in Python are fairly costly -- on the Raspberry Pi I'm using, it can take upward of a millisecond to take the stack traces and whatnot involved in generating an exception -- that's a high cost to pay for something that might be happening frequently. But in this case, to maintain compatibility, it's probably better to throw the timeout exception?)

There's an open question about whether success should be returned immediately when any bytes are written, or whether it should keep trying until the entire timeout interval is consumed (or the entire string is written) -- there are pros and cons both ways. Either way, implementing those semantics seems relatively straightforward.

This issue is much more subtle than it appears. I'm not exactly sure this is the right fix and I don't think we should take this fix for 5.0. Its an issue that requires quite a bit of discussion.

We chose to make predictions using the most information we have during prediction time. The default option for prediction is imputing and when features are not present during predict time, we impute.  For a single example, if a single feature is missing, inputing is probably the right thing to do. In batch mode, if one example in one row is missing, imputing a single value is the right thing to do. 

When you add these two things up and want a consistent API, the current API is the only choice. The user always has the option of turning off imputation in all the predict calls but that's not the default option.

This code block causes problems.

```python
    # this code is needed to handle the new location.tz format string
    try:
        utc_offset = pytz.timezone(location.tz).utcoffset(
            time[0]).total_seconds() / 3600.
    except ValueError:
        utc_offset = time.tzinfo.utcoffset(time[0]).total_seconds() / 3600.
    pvl_logger.debug('utc_offset={}'.format(utc_offset))
```

It creates a fixed ``utc_offset`` based the first element of the DatetimeIndex. ``utc_offset`` should instead be an array so that it can handle a change from DST to non-DST. I believe that the original code suffers from the same problem.

To fix it, I suggest using [``pvlib.tools.localize``](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/tools.py#L106), and the ``hours`` attribute of the returned object in place of ``UnivHr = DecHours + utc_offset - .5``.

I think this also removes the need for

```python
    Hour = time.hour
    Minute = time.minute
    Second = time.second
```

I guess the question is what should we expect from a validator?. 

The spec doesn't seem to allow mixed case `true`, `false`, `WITHIN`, etc. It seems like an easy fix in the lexer. It actually makes getting good coverage of the lexer easier.

As @f0k mentioned above, there are three possibilities for `(k, h, w)` convolutions, depending on the shape `(p, q, r)` of the filter (assuming `mode='valid'` semantics):

* If `p==1`, you get a stack of 2d convolutions, which are already supported/can be mimicked by 2d + reshape.
* If `p==k`, you can sum out a stack of 2d convolutions to achieve the volumetric convolution.
* If `1<p<k`, then weird stuff can happen unless there's a meaningful topology to the third dimension.  (Red->Green->Blue maybe isn't that meaningful, but a time dimension might be.)

So in light of the above, what do folks think about providing a class that fakes the first two options (or just the second one, since it's the most interesting)?  It seems to me that this should still compile down to reasonably efficient code (compared to theano's existing 3d conv support), and cover the interesting use cases.

I know you like bullet lists, but I think the docstring is quite readable now (each sentence describes one case, without excessive vertical space). Anyway, what we're discussing is that the implementation doesn't match it (and cannot match it if we don't distinguish different kinds of iterables).

I think if we have layers that return multiple outputs, it might be best if only layers that can accept multiple inputs can be stacked on top of them. This would be a new 'type' of layer that we haven't implemented yet. In fact, with this approach we might not need any changes to the existing code base at all.

Note that this is different from e.g. MergeLayers, which take inputs from multiple **layers**, instead of accepting multiple inputs from a single layer.

... but we should also be wary of introducing too much complexity. Perhaps some of these use cases can be solved in simpler ways.

I don't think usercorn supports running binaries with shared objects of different architectures... I guess it *could* be done (by translating the argument registers from one unicorn instance, under one arch, to another instance, under another arch, and mapping their memories to be the same), but I'm 99% sure this isn't functionality that usercorn supports right now. And it'd probably be easier to do that in plain angr, because the calling convention helpers would at least help a bit with the job of translating the arguments. There'll be plenty of other issues due to assumptions of a single architecture across the entire binary, though.

But what would be the practical purpose of this capability?

The top level clemory, `Loader.memory`, is the whole address space as it appears in a debugger. Each backer memory should be the layout as it appears in readelf. Both these assumptions are very strongly baked into CLE. I now realize that this will cause problems with non-zero-based pic-libraries, since clemory will probably complain that there are overlapping backers. Two possible solutions come to mind:

- Change clemory's overlap detection to use the "minimum mapped address" of another clemory. This will complicate the lookup code drastically, I think.
- Have each backer have two clemories - one that is the memory of the process starting at address 0, regardless of what the base address of the program is supposed to be, and one that is the former clemory mapped at the correct address for where the elf says it should go. The former clemory would be what is mapped into the global clemory, at address `obj.get_min_addr()`.

Hi @SalahuddinAsh 

It goes like this. DK-Python will run on almost any platform that runs platform - including RPi. 

DK-SITL is a framework that launches ArduPilot SITL binaries. While DK-SITL itself will run on almost any platform, we only supply ArduPilot binaries for x86 platforms - so basically your Windows 10, Mac OS X or Linux PC using Intel/AMD chips. If you want to use DK-SITL on ARM platforms like RPi it won't be able to download the binaries. YOu can however build SITL natively to create the needed binaries and then DK-SITL can launch those.

The examples use DK-Python to connect to a real or simulated vehicle using a specified "connection" for where on the network the vehicle is located. If you don't specify any connection then the examples use DK-SITL to launch SITL and connect to it.

If you want to start testing on RPi, then you would have to run SITL on another machine on the same network, then connect the RPi to that. 

For your case, I would step away from your RPi altogether and run the examples on a desktop. When you're happy with the behaviour, push the scripts to RPi and run them talking to your autopilot.

Hope that helps.

There should be no problem to use more than one camera, but you should be aware of USB3 bandwidth limitations. Ideally, you would want to connect several USB controllers to PCI-E slots on your motherboard and connect 2 cameras to each. 
When two cameras share a USB3 hub (and sometimes even the USB controller) they will inevitably start loosing frames in somewhat unpredictable way. 
We had very good experience with [Renesas usb 3.0 controllers](https://www.renesas.com/en-us/solutions/key-technology/connectivity-wired/usb.html), but other brands can also work well.

Looking directly at the infrared input can give us a clue about what is going on. I'm concerned that some of the infrared images are getting over or under saturated and the auto-exposure is either disabled or not doing its job. 
ZR300 camera is generating depth from stereo. This means that if the infrared image has a lot of details, the projector is optional. If the image is "flat", the projector is artificially injecting more details into the scene, and it is done in a way that should not be destructive. However, if the extra details are not visible in the infrared image (either they are influenced by SR300 patterns, or the image has low contrast or is very bright or dark) then you will only get proper depth on objects edges.

Pylint 1.4.3 is old. It was [released](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/releases/tag/pylint-1.4.3) on March 14, 2015. The solution to this issue is to use newer Python packages. Are you using the packages provided by your package manager? I recommend that you avoid the packages provided by your package manager, and use a Python virtualenv instead. Thankfully, it's easy to use a virtualenv, as Pulp Smash and all of its dependencies are pure Python packages. The procedure for doing this is outlined in Pulp Smash's documentation, notably the [Installation](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html) section. If using Python 2, it goes like this:

```sh
virtualenv -p python2 env
source env/bin/activate
git clone https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash.git
cd pulp-smash
pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt
```

All of Pulp Smash's dependencies are installed into the virtualenv, and Pulp Smash itself is installed in "develop mode," meaning that when changes are made to the Pulp Smash source code, those changes immediately show up in installed package.

To exit the virtualenv, run `deactivate`. At this point, the virtualenv can be destroyed with `rm -rf env`. For more information, read the fantastic documentation.

By the way, Travis' logs show which packages are installed into its virtualenv. See, for example, https://travis-ci.org/PulpQE/pulp-smash/jobs/148656163

ppo2 needs fixed batch size when dealing with recurrent policies. Could you provide more details on the failure? I suspect it has something to do with number of environments simulated in parallel larger than one, and then trying to call model.step() with obs coming from a single env. 
In this case, you need to re-load the weights into a model that has been created for a single environment. For instance: 
```
# train the model with 8 environments in parallel:
python -m baselines.run --alg=ppo2 --env=PongNoFrameskip-v4 --num_env=8 --save_path=~/models/pongmodel --num_timesteps=1e6 
# load the model and render single environment:
python -m baselines.run --alg=ppo2 --env=PongNoFrameskip-v4 --load_path=~/models/pongmodel --num_timesteps=0 --play
```

To make it more versatile, those form-based mutations should provide easy ways to override both the input and the output.

> I have encountered a case in which I passed ~/Desktop/logdir as the flag value for a model, […] the script literally made a directory path called ~/Desktop/logdir in my current working directory.

This means that your shell did not perform tilde expansion on the logdir value. There are two possibilities as to why:

 1. You wrote `–logdir "~/Desktop/logdir"`, with quotes. In this case, the solution is simple: don’t quote the argument.
 2. You wrote `–logdir=~/Desktop/logdir`, using the `–foo=bar` argument form. In this case, the solution is also simple: write `–logdir ~/Desktop/logdir`. This is one reason why `–foo=bar`-style arguments are inferior. Another is tab completion. The fundamental problem is that you are using one shell “word” to contain two different ideas: first, the symbol `–logdir`, and second, the string `~/Desktop`. This is simply not how the shell was designed to work, and trying to force it to work this way is just not a good idea. (I’ll PR against all our docs right now to fix this.)

Thinking about this, handling the failure case  may require some care with the new model specially for handling of structs and types that contain more than one value.

When the `spinel_datatype_vunpack_in_place` is called we don't yet know if the received spinel frame (content of `aBuffer`) is valid and can be parsed correctly for the given format. We need to ensure that for a failed case, the passed-in variables remain unchanged.  This become tricky if the format contains structs or more than one value, as the `unpack` may be able to parse/decode some of the values but not all.

The existing model/API addresses this as follows:
- The caller upacks the content into local variables (gets pointers into the buffer where the data is without copying it over),
- Checks the status of upack and ensure the operation succeeded 
- Only if successful, the caller copies/moves the content where they need to go.

Wit new model, we may need to keep a copy of original values of parameters sent to `int GetProp(spinel_key_t aKey, const char* aFormat, ...);` to be able to revert back in case of failure...

With current implementation, when MAC is stopped/disbaled, if `SendFrameRequest(Sender sender)` is called (by higher layer), the `SendFrameRequest()` will succeed but `PerformOperation` will take no action and the `sender`'s callbacks are never called. 

I think we need to return an error from `SendFrameRequest()` when MAC is disabled. 

Alternative is to accept a new sender given in `SendFrameRequest(sender)` but then invoke the sender's callback with some error status indicating MAC is stopped. I prefer returning an error from `SendFrameRequest()`

You are correct that the API will look very similar.  However the new interface will really be a superset of the existing otPlatRadio interface.  You have already begun that process by increasing the otPlatRadio API's for features that OT does not use.  

GetChannel
GetPanID
GetShortAddress
GetExtendedAddress 

These are useful API's for OT 'on-host' systems where the two systems can get out of sync from one another, but not on a OT 'on-device' system.  Also, I suspect this is just the beginning as I imagine features like wake-on-wireless and data poll off-load becoming necessary for OT 'on-host' systems so that the host can power down when possible and leave the simple redundant work to the device. Do you intend to just add these features to every platform whether they need it or not?

You have also increased the functionality of ncp_base in ways that most customers will not use.  Everything in this PR could be written such that it can be compiled out for those customers who do not need it, which is everyone except Microsoft.

OpenThread has no dependencies on ncp_base.  We can build a system that doesn't have ncp_base at all and in fact we do have such systems in development.  We should have made otPlatRadio such that it has no dependencies on OpenThread and could be built without OpenThread present in the system.  Then otPlat could be integrated into images both with and without OT.  One problem is that OpenThread defines the interface. Instead we should have adopted a more DLL approach where each module gets to define its own interface and the callers must adapt to it.  Then we could get a way with one interface for both OT and ncp_base.  Of course we would also want to configure each module so that it only compiles in what is required.  

My suggestion of having a new radio interface is a shortcut to avoid creating this new separate radio module.

This has gotten way too big, but just let me finish with this thought.  A solution that can be compile time configured for OT 'on-device' operation AND/OR OT 'on-host' operation is what I want to get too.  I don't want a solution that is always configured for OT 'on-device' AND OT 'on-host'.

As for the mapping between CDP and LLDP capabilities, here's a first analysis of it.

### LLDP

From IEEE document about LLDP

Bit   | Cisco Code | Juniper Code | Capability
----- | ---------- | ------------ | ------------
0x001 | `O`        |              | Other
0x002 | `P`        |              | Repeater
0x004 | `B`        |              | MAC Bridge component
0x008 | `W`        |              | 802.11 Access Point (AP)
0x010 | `R`        | Router       | Router
0x020 | `T`        | Telephone    | Telephone
0x040 | `C`        |              | DOCSIS cable device
0x080 | `S`        |              | Station Only
0x100 |            |              | C-VLAN component
0x200 |            |              | S-VLAN component
0x400 |            |              | Two-port MAC Relay component


### CDP

From https://wiki.wireshark.org/CDP

Bit  | Cisco Code | Capability
---- | ---------- | ------------
0x01 | `R`        | Router
0x02 | `T`        | Transparant Bidge
0x04 | `B`        | Source Route Bridge
0x08 | `S`        | Switch
0x10 | `H`        | Host 
0x20 | `I`        | IGMP capable
0x40 | `r`        | Repeater
0x80 | `P`        | Phone (Not documented but visible in show command on IOS)

Thanks for starting this!

Here are a few thoughts/opinions I have in regards to tests for verifying the documentation build:
* They probably should NOT be part of our normal unit test suite
    * They are NOT unit tests of our software - they are system-level acceptance tests of our documentation
* We should put them in a different directory than under **tests**, perhaps in **docs/tests**
* They only need to be run on one OS and one version of Python
    * Preferably the same as will be used by readthedocs.org to build our documentation, or at least close
* Should not be run with code coverage analysis as there would be no benefit and it would only slow things down

One possibility might be to tweak the VSTS build to add a step which installs the prerequisites for building the documentation and then runs the test to validate documentation build.  The VSTS build doesn't use ``tox`` and is really just a sequence of shell scripts.

But you are much more familiar with``tox`` than I am, so you can probably think of something clever ;-)

As long as you are working with C++, this works, and it does the redirection directly in the C++ code. Since libraries usually don't change IO, this either works if `cout`/`cerr` is used, or it doesn't if `printf` and raw file descriptors are used for a given library. This is much simpler and completely portable, and can be added in C++ instead of wrapping in Python.

The reason why this is happening is that when you renew a certificate you are actually issuing an entirely new one. If it has different domain names listed than the original certificate (like in your case) then the Let's Encrypt CA doesn't know that they're actually certificates that Certbot views as the same. 
This is why you got an expiration notice, because the initial certificates are expiring, and Let's Encrypt doesn't see newer versions of them (instead you have these new different certificates).
I don't think we can fix this in Certbot so I'm going to close this issue, but if you want more insight on this I'd suggest you post on community.letsencrypt.org

I'm afraid it isn't as simple as switching the compiler for this project (`PySCIPOpt`). My understanding ist that we are essentially dealing with three independently compiled parts: SCIPOpt, Python and PySCIPOpt as a bridge between them. I'm fairly sure that the compiler version used for Python dictates which compiler must be used for PySCIPOpt as it contains C/Cython-Code. This is fairly well documented [here](https://wiki.python.org/moin/WindowsCompilers) and [here](https://github.com/cython/cython/wiki/CythonExtensionsOnWindows). As I stated in my previous post I suspect that this constraint may also apply to binaries that are only indirectly included via Cython (i.e. SCIPOpt).

AFAIK Visual Studio 2017 and 2015 use the same Visual Compiler VC14 which is to use with Python 3.5 and 3.6, so that should not make any difference. For Python 2.7 the recommended compiler is `Microsoft Visual C++ Compiler for Python 2.7` which is included in the 2015 AppVeyor image. So as far as PySCIPOpt goes AppVeyor should be setup correctly as I already allowed for this.

We could indeed attempt to also build SCIP within AppVeyor using the "correct" compiler versions as defined by the Python version. I don't know how difficult that would be. I don't think that total build time is that much of a problem for an automated build, but the last time I tried building SCIP under Windows (~3-5 years ago) it was a pain and I don't think I succeeded in the end.

In summary, Canvas treats assignments in a group with a 0% weight (if weighted grading is enabled) and assignments with zero possible points as non-existent. In this case, both were true, causing the grade-related fields to be `null`. You can fix this by ensuring that your assignments are in groups that are weighted properly (> 0%) or by making sure that your assignments have > 0 possible points.

From my own testing, I discovered that the string `True` and the string `False` are not evaluated correctly by Canvas (in this case).

The requests library sends `'True'` and `'False'` when it encounters a boolean, so this would explain the behavior. We can hotfix this issue by converting boolean values to strings ourselves before we fire off a request, but I think it's worth starting a dialogue with Canvas about the inconsistency we've observed.

I agree with @mehrdadn that `super(type(self), self)` isn't what you want. Naming the class (or better yet, in Python3, using `super()`) is significant here.

In python, `B` refer to `class B` before any decoration. This indeed creates an issue for replacing decorators (returning a different class than their argument, as opposed to returning the same class modified), which is one of the reasons they should be discouraged. The case of `ray.remote` is particularly tricky, since the returned class doesn't have the original MRO (it seems to be derived from `object`), and the `foo` decorator above won't behave as intended.

Until and unless `ray.remote` preserves the original MRO, I'd say that decorating an actor is unsupported, so shouldn't matter for this issue. As for `bar`, if it behaves as expected, then it shouldn't care that `B` refers to `class B`.

So apparently Python implements some very basic, non-standardised version of the syslog protocol (which I assume is only accepted by the servers for historical reasons).

**ident** is a proper field in the protocol that allows you to separate different programs from each other. Putting it in the message part is a hack which does not achieve the same goal. If Python wants to do dumb things, then that's up to them. But I don't think we should play along.

If we want to fix this then have a look at RFC 5424 and do a subclass of `SysLogHandler` that actually sends the proper stuff. Or start using the Python `syslog` module. But that only works on Unix.

import asyncio
from ipykernel.eventloops import register_integration

@register_integration('asyncio')
def loop_asyncio(kernel):
    '''Start a kernel with asyncio event loop support.'''
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    def kernel_handler():
        loop.call_soon(kernel.do_one_iteration)
        loop.call_later(kernel._poll_interval, kernel_handler)

    loop.call_soon(kernel_handler)
    try:
        if not loop.is_running():
            loop.run_forever()

Some ideas for names: `spinoff` vs `startup` (or just `start`, `spinoff` vs `incubate`, `spawn` vs `incubate`

I like how `incubate` emphasises that this is a blocking call. I'm not a big fan of it being 8 characters, given that it's the one people should maybe be using more often, or at least as often.

No idea whether other people would read `spinoff` as suggesting a lack of connection between the two tasks. Also no idea whether these weird names mean anything to non-native speakers, though this is a core enough api that it might be ok to spend a little bit of weirdness budget here.

> For some reason, only on PyPy nightly, joining the thread blocks forever randomly.

So the traceback shows the watchdog thread is blocked in `event.wait`, and it's blocked for a minute, so probably a call without a timeout. Probably the one on line 42 of `_watchdog.py`:

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/753753be65de75c8dc1369d3738ed01045fbaa8d/trio/_core/_watchdog.py#L42

And the main thread is stuck in `join`, called from inside our `stop` method.

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/753753be65de75c8dc1369d3738ed01045fbaa8d/trio/_core/_watchdog.py#L81-L84

That's weird, it looks like our `stop` method sets the event, so how could the watchdog be blocked waiting for it? Assuming `Event` actually works, the only way this could happen is if someone calls `clear`...

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/753753be65de75c8dc1369d3738ed01045fbaa8d/trio/_core/_watchdog.py#L34-L42

Ah crud, the watchdog thread calls `clear` just before it goes to sleep. So if we happen to call `stop` *just* in that interval between the `if self._stopped` and `self._notify_event.clear`, then we'll deadlock. I'm not 100% sure that this is the problem we're seeing on pypy, but it's certainly *a* problem in the algorithm I suggested, and could explain it.

Man I hate threads.

I suspect that instead of messing around with `Event` like this, we want to switch to using a `Condition` (like how `threading.Event` is implemented, if you look), so that we can atomically check the stopped flag and counter values before sleeping.

Usually each of our test files is run as separate binaries, so different flags from different test files don't interact. (This is done via [bazel](https://bazel.build/).)

In your case, each test file is imported `master_test`, so you can think of each test is a *library*, and only the `master_test` is a *binary*. It isn't a good case to define conflict flags in *libraries* shared in a single *binary*.

Do you need to specify the flags defined in individual test files on the *command line*?
* If not, they sound more like *global variables*, not *flags* to me.
* If yes, then I assume they are shared across the tests and only parsed inside the `master_test`. So I suggest to define the flag in `master_test` only, and pass the *flag values* to each individual tests. Or, as @gpshead pointed out, you can use `absl.testing.flagsaver` in your tests, but you still define the same flag in *one* place. Example:

```python
# File: shared_flags.py
flags.DEFINE_string('encoder', None, 'The encoder to use')
```

```python
# File: test_a.py
def test_a():
  encoder = create(FLAGS.encoder)
  ...
```

```python
# File: test_b.py
def test_b():
  encoder = create(FLAGS.encoder)
  ...
```

```python
# File: master_test.py
from absl import app
from absl import flags
from absl.testing import flagsaver
import shared_flags  # Import to define --encoder.
import test_a
import test_b

FLAGS = flags.FLAGS

def main(argv):
  test_cases = [('encoder a', test_a.test_a), ('encoder b', test_b.test_b)]
  for encoder, test_func in test_funcs:
    with flagsaver.flagsaver():
      FLAGS.encoder = encoder
      test_func()

if __name__ == '__main__':
  app.run(main)
```

It looks like a great start! Also, good job on creating the tests.

I have a few suggestions:

1) I like to think of the `%` as a command prefix, and not as part of the keyword. In other words, I think the syntax would be better as `%from foo import bar`, instead of `%import`

2) I can see why you chose to have `import` and `rel_import` as two separate rules (and same with `from`), but I still think it probably makes more sense to have only `import` and `from`, and allow `import_args` (probably needs a better name) to be either relative or absolute. I think it will make the grammar shorter, and also simplify the post-processing.

3) There is some repetition in the code you added to `load_grammar`. I think you can merge these two paths into a single one. But if not, then move the repeated code to its own function. (aka DRY)

It's taking me a while to grasp all the concepts so I can start adapting the official glsl ebnf grammar to lark, I must to admit all this stuff isn't easy... plus, it's been like 10 years since I took all these courses about compilers, automatas, FSM ... nowadays, I forgot all of it, I don't remember not even one single theorem from the zillion existing ones ;D

Btw, do you think it'd be very tricky to give support to something similar to this syntax:

    grammar = '''
        start: jump_statement+
        jump_statement : 
            | CONTINUE SEMICOLON
            | BREAK SEMICOLON
            | RETURN SEMICOLON
            | DISCARD SEMICOLON // Fragment shader only
        CONTINUE : "continue"
        SEMICOLON : ";"
        BREAK : "break"
        RETURN : "return"
        DISCARD : "discard"
        %import common.WS
        %ignore WS
    '''

instead the current syntax:

    grammar = '''
        start: jump_statement+
        jump_statement : CONTINUE SEMICOLON
            | BREAK SEMICOLON
            | RETURN SEMICOLON
            | DISCARD SEMICOLON // Fragment shader only
        CONTINUE : "continue"
        SEMICOLON : ";"
        BREAK : "break"
        RETURN : "return"
        DISCARD : "discard"
        %import common.WS
        %ignore WS

So I didn't have much time to look at this and stew on in, but I'm definitely interested in this overall direction. 

With this approach I think that the notion of the environment creation should be a self-describing attribute of a pipeline similar to an InputDefinition. It should be part of the metadata system in some respect. Call it EnvironmentDefinition (although I am not sold on the name "Environment"). Like InputDefinition it specifies it's own args. 

Now this begs the question of what object EnvironmentDefinition should hang off of. Without changing anything else we would add a set of EnvironmentDefinitions to a SolidDefinition. However that would add a lot of replication. So this would naturally hang off of a Pipeline. But pipelines could be multiple environment? In which case there might be a missing abstraction to group together solids in a different dimension.

Whatever object it hangs off, there should be 1 or more environments available, and they might take different parameters. SqlLite for local development and some other db for prod & integration testing, as an example. 

So at minimum I think there should be a way to describe all the environments available for a particular computation and then describe the arguments for those environments. I think you'll naturally want to add a new grouping concept to group solids together that take the same set of possible environments, and that grouping may be different from pipeline.
